vintage_guidance: Recent vintages 2020–2023 widely available; 2020/2022 researched. Estate is the largest 1er+Grand Cru holder in Chablis; acquired by Domaines Barons de Rothschild Lafite in 2024.
selection_notes: Reference Chablis 1er Cru in steely, mineral, low-new-oak style — pairs as the cool-Burgundy foil to oak-driven Côte de Beaune Chardonnay (Meursault/Puligny) and to riper New World Chardonnays (Sonoma/Margaret River). Useful as the benchmark in any single-variety Chardonnay quality or origin flight.

tasting_profile.appearance: Pale lemon with glowing green-gold highlights; brilliant clarity. Jasper Morris notes 'a glowing lemon and lime colour' (2023 vintage) and 'pale but glowing' with 'as much yellow as white but balanced' (2022). Medium viscosity consistent with 12.5% ABV.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Intensely mineral-driven nose with prominent white flower aromas (acacia blossom), citrus confit, lemon zest, and orchard fruit. Distinctive Kimmeridgian seashore notes: iodine, shellfish, oyster shell, and saline/algae character. Secondary notes of crushed limestone, wet stones, oatmeal, and chalky reduction. Burghound highlights 'white flowers, prominent mineral reduction and plenty of seashore elements'; CellarTracker notes 'crushed limestone, sappy orchard fruits, chalk dust, salty sea air with a little crushed oyster funk.'
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Medium to full-bodied, satiny and layered with excellent mid-palate density. Crisp orchard fruit and citrus (lemon oil, citron confit) supported by a chiselled white fruit intensity. Sappy texture with evident minerality and stoniness. William Kelley (Robert Parker, 94 pts) describes it as 'fleshy and seamless, with fine depth at the core.' Finish is long, driving, firm, and impeccably balanced with pungent stoniness and good acidity at the back.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Acid: high, racy, providing excellent tension and backbone. Body: medium to medium-plus. Alcohol: 12.5%, modest and well-integrated. Tannin: n/a (white). Finish: long to very long, with persistent mineral and stony character. Oak is restrained -- partial seasoned French oak only, no new oak -- adding subtle textural richness without overt oak flavour.
sources: William Kelley, Robert Parker Wine Advocate (94 pts, 2020 vintage)
Jasper Morris MW, Inside Burgundy (92-95 pts, 2022 vintage; 94-96 pts, 2023 vintage)

vintage_guidance: Released annually; 2019/2022 researched. A flagship southern-Burgundy Chardonnay and the largest-volume Mâcon-Lugny on world markets.
selection_notes: Inexpensive, ripe-ish southern Burgundy Chardonnay anchor. Useful as the value-tier foil in any Chardonnay quality ladder or Burgundy regional spread (Mâcon → Chablis → Côte de Beaune village). Provides the unoaked / minimally oaked end of the Burgundy spectrum without going to Chablis.

tasting_profile.appearance: Pale yellow to pale gold with brilliant clarity and youthful vibrancy. The 2023 tech sheet describes 'pale yellow in colour'; the 2024 vintage shows 'a brilliant pale gold colour.' Light to medium viscosity consistent with the fresh, early-drinking style at 13% ABV.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Fresh and fruit-forward nose centred on citrus (grapefruit, green lemon) and orchard fruit (ripe apple, pear). The 2023 vintage reveals aromas of muscatel and brioche with yellow peach notes. The 2024 shows floral, expressive aromas of acacia and honeysuckle with delicate vanilla. Older vintages add subtle honey and nutty notes. Sommeliers Choice Awards (91 pts, 2022) notes 'ripe apple with a hint of spice, citrus, orchard fruits, and rocky minerals.'
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Ample and round on the palate with generous stone fruit and citrus character. Yellow peach (2023) and yellow fruit (2024) dominate, with muscat-like aromatics on the mid-palate. Medium-bodied with a suave, approachable texture. Best Buy Liquors describes it as 'opulent, rich, with a flowery bouquet and lovely honey, apple and nutty flavours.' Finish shows nice freshness and good length for the appellation, though not extended. Wine Spectator (2023) calls it 'fresh and rather steely.'
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Acid: medium to medium-plus, providing freshness without austerity. Body: medium. Alcohol: 13%, well-balanced. Tannin: n/a (white). Finish: medium length with clean, fresh citrus-mineral close. No oak influence -- 100% stainless steel fermentation and ageing for 8-10 months. Cellaring potential: 2-3 years.
sources: Maison Louis Latour official tech sheet (2023 and 2024 vintages)
Wine on Sale / Best Buy Liquors retailer tasting notes (2022 and 2024 vintages)

vintage_guidance: Recent vintages 2021–2024 in market; 2024 researched. Village-level cuvée drawing from across the appellation's 26 climats.
selection_notes: Côte de Beaune village white anchor in the textbook négociant-domaine style — barrel-fermented and barrel-aged with modest new oak. Sits between Chablis (steely, low oak) and the Mâconnais (ripe, unoaked) inside a Burgundy white quality ladder, and contrasts oak-driven New World Chardonnays in cross-country flights.

tasting_profile.appearance: Bright, shiny lemon-yellow of medium intensity with hints of a gold hue. Social Vignerons describes the 2014 as 'a bright and shiny lemon-yellow color of medium intensity, although made deeper by hints of a gold hue.' Good clarity and moderate viscosity consistent with barrel-fermented Burgundy at 13% ABV.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Noble and distinguished aromas combining citrus (lemon, candied lemon), stone fruit (peach, apricot), and delicate florals (acacia blossom, lily, elderflower). Pronounced mineral character with toasted and nutty notes from barrel fermentation -- sour dough, toasted nuts, buttery brioche. Subtle oak spice and hints of caramel provide depth. ReserveBar notes 'beautiful minerality and toasted scents'; Social Vignerons highlights 'punchy notes of dough and toasted nuts as dominant aromas.' Wilson Daniels (Esprit Leflaive) describes 'ripe stone fruit and citrus with subtle saline notes, flint, and fresh white flowers.'
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Dry, mineral-driven palate with crisp acidity providing sustained tension. Oily-enough texture from lees ageing, yet lean and focused. Bursts of subtle tropical fruit (pineapple, mango) backed by rich ripe peach, combined with powerful spicy oak notes. ReserveBar notes 'baked apple, peach, spiced pear, subtle pineapple and mango flavours, lemony acidity.' CellarTracker (2022) praises 'all the right peach/pear notes with crisp acidity and medium to long finish. Extremely balanced.' The finish is rather long and layered on sweet/savoury flavours with excellent persistence.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Acid: medium-plus to high, providing crisp minerality and tension throughout. Body: medium, relatively light which aids approachability and purity of fruit expression. Alcohol: 13%. Tannin: n/a (white). Finish: medium-long with good persistence. Oak: 100% French oak (circa 20% new) for 12 months, plus 4-6 months stainless steel -- adds textural richness and subtle spice without dominating.
sources: Olivier Leflaive official website (2021 vintage tasting note)
Wilson Daniels / Esprit Leflaive tech sheet

vintage_guidance: Recent vintages 2021–2023 in market; 2022/2023 researched. Old-vine village cuvée from 9.82 ha across ~20 plots planted 1945–1969.
selection_notes: Côte de Nuits village Pinot Noir benchmark in a powerful, structured Gevrey style. Anchors any Pinot Noir cross-country flight (Burgundy vs Oregon vs Central Otago) and the Burgundy red side of any quality ladder. The Vieilles Vignes selection makes it punch above a basic village in concentration without crossing into 1er Cru pricing.

tasting_profile.appearance: Deep, clear ruby-red hue. Wine Spectator (2023 vintage) notes 'a pale ruby color' suggesting the 2023 is lighter; the Domaine describes 'a deep, clear ruby-red hue' as the house norm. Good clarity and moderate-plus viscosity typical of concentrated village Gevrey.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Exceptionally intense and aromatic nose revealing ripe dark berries (black cherry, blackcurrant, boysenberry), sweet berries, and animalistic nuances. Robert Parker (2018, 90 pts) describes 'aromas of sweet berries, black truffle, and spices, framed by a subtle hint of oak.' Wine Spectator (2023, 92 pts) highlights 'cherry, strawberry, rose, mineral and spice flavours' with 'purity and finesse.' The Domaine's own notes emphasise 'ripe dark berries with animalistic nuances.' Earthy, iron-rich mineral undertones from clay-marl soils.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Medium to full-bodied, fleshy, and enveloping with a concentrated, rich core. The 2018 Robert Parker note describes 'a concentrated, rich, and reserved core built around velvety tannins and lively acidity.' JJ Buckley (2022) notes 'intensely flavoured, featuring cherry, raspberry, boysenberry and earth flavours.' Wine Spectator (2023) praises 'excellent length and the impression of approachability' with 'deftly balanced' structure. Sweet spice, fleshy palate texture, notes of rich soil and black truffle. The Domaine highlights 'rich, powerful flavours' on the palate.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Acid: medium-plus, lively, providing freshness and lift. Body: medium to full. Alcohol: 13%. Tannin: medium-plus, velvety to fine-grained with a light powdery dusting on the finish. Finish: long, well-defined, with lingering earth and spice. Oak: 14 months French oak, less than one-third new, with regular stirring -- subtle, not dominant.
sources: Robert Parker / Wine Advocate (90 pts, 2018 vintage)
Wine Spectator (92 pts, 2023 vintage; 93 pts, 2018 vintage)
Wilson Daniels / Domaine Faiveley official tech sheet
JJ Buckley retailer tasting note (2022 vintage)

vintage_guidance: Annual release; 2021/2022/2024 researched. Estate cuvée from the Drouhin family's Dundee Hills vineyard, made in the same Joseph Drouhin Burgundian style since 1988.
selection_notes: Oregon Pinot anchor in the most overtly Burgundian Willamette house style — useful precisely because it can be confused with village Burgundy in a blind. Pairs in cross-country Pinot flights against Gevrey-Chambertin (Old World, more iron/earth) and Central Otago (riper, more concentrated New World). Also a strong choice for a 'guess the country' anti-overlink question.

tasting_profile.appearance: Radiant red ruby hue. Veronique Drouhin-Boss describes the 2022 as displaying 'a radiant, red ruby hue.' The 2021 is noted as 'dark red colored' (Ken's Wine Guide). Medium viscosity; the wine appears lighter and more translucent than its 14.1% ABV might suggest.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Tight and delightful gathering of tart cherries, peony, pomegranate, red fruits, and wood spice (2022 tech sheet). The 2021 is 'bursting with black cherry and ripe sour cherry tones, with hints of dried violets and lightly roasted coffee beans.' Wine Enthusiast (94 pts, 2022) notes 'briary raspberries, graphite, earth and coffee with cream.' James Suckling (94 pts, 2022) finds 'spiced cherries, aged tangerine peel, crushed spices and nutshells, some licorice notes.' John Gilman (93 pts, 2022) describes 'cherries, sweet beetroot, woodsmoke, gamebird, soil tones, gentle stem tones and cedary oak.'
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Balanced and clear acidity with customary elegance. The 2022 is 'medium to full-bodied, structured and polished with a firm backbone of very fine tannins and earthy undertones' (James Suckling). Wine Enthusiast notes it 'feels light on its feet, with a nice balance between gentle acidity and velvety tannins.' Ken's Wine Guide (2021, 94 pts) finds 'medium-bodied with integrated medium acidity, nicely balanced, smooth, and savoury' with 'black raspberry and light loam, notes of cherry-cola.' MyWinePal describes 'smooth and round with sweet red fruit, spice, and subtle oak.' The finish is lingering with fine tannins and earthy persistence.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Acid: medium-plus, well-integrated, providing Burgundian elegance. Body: medium to medium-full. Alcohol: 13.5-14.1% depending on vintage. Tannin: fine-grained, velvety, well-structured but not aggressive. Finish: medium-long to long with lingering earthy and fruit character. Oak: French oak (Burgundy coopers), about 20% new, for 12-14 months -- adds subtle cedar and spice framing.
sources: Domaine Drouhin Oregon official tech sheet / Veronique Boss-Drouhin tasting note (2022 vintage)
Wine Enthusiast (94 pts, Editors' Choice, 2022 vintage)
John Gilman / View from the Cellar (93 pts, 2022 vintage)

vintage_guidance: Annual release; 2021–2024 vintages researched. Blend of Felton Road's four Bannockburn estate vineyards (Elms, Macmuir, Calvert, Cornish Point); fully Demeter biodynamic since 2010.
selection_notes: Reference Central Otago Pinot at benchmark quality. Riper, more sun-saturated than Burgundy or Oregon while still showing fine tannins and savoury complexity from old-clone biodynamic estate fruit. Use as the warmest-climate end of any Pinot Noir cross-country flight (Burgundy → Oregon → Central Otago) and as the New World benchmark to test whether a candidate pushes too quickly to California or Australia.

tasting_profile.appearance: Medium-deep ruby with good intensity and clarity. The wine presents with more concentration and depth of colour than Burgundy equivalents, reflecting Central Otago's greater sun exposure. Screw-cap closure.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Dense and richly aromatic with dark cherries, wild raspberries, mulberries, and blood plums. Exotic spice notes moving towards sumac, peppercorns, and Indian spices. Wineanorak (2023, 94 pts) describes 'wonderfully chiselled aromatics with sweet red cherries and plums, and a nice spicy, almost peppery twist.' Wine Advocate (2021, 92 pts) finds 'densely packed and powerful, with a cavalcade of red fruits, exotic spice and peppercorns.' Winepilot (2024, 97 pts) notes 'dark cherries, wild raspberries, mulberries, graphite, Indian spices and well-polished blood plums.' Wine Enthusiast (2019) highlights 'brambly berries, tree sap, graphite, baking spice, mushrooms and florals.' Saratoga Wine (2024) adds 'strawberries, orange peel, sandalwood and potpourri.'
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Spicy, exciting, and full with ripe fruit through the finish and a lick of raw cocoa. The 2023 has 'a rich, sweet core with bold, almost meaty cherry and raspberry fruit and some soft wild strawberry richness -- very seductive and more-ish, showing massive drinkability' (Wineanorak). Vinous (2022, 91 pts) describes it as 'pure, refined and lifted with a silky, smooth texture that caresses the mouth... sweet raspberry and cherry meet a finely tuned herby character.' Winepilot notes 'layer after layer of succulent fruit' with 'a foxy undergrowth character.' Finishes with spicy twist, cedar, and appetising textures.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Acid: medium-plus, well-placed, providing freshness and lift. Body: medium to medium-plus, with notable concentration. Alcohol: 13.5-14%. Tannin: fine yet firm, sometimes chunky in richer vintages; talc-like and appetising in texture. Finish: medium-long to long with scented spice, cedar, and a sense of tension. Oak: 13 months French oak, circa 25% new -- well-integrated, adding polish without masking fruit. Biodynamic/organic farming shows in the purity and energy of the fruit.
sources: Wine Advocate / Joe Czerwinski (92 pts, 2021 vintage)
Vinous / Rebecca Gibb MW (91 pts, 2022 vintage)

vintage_guidance: Use recent vintage; 2019 (70% Cabernet Sauvignon, 24% Merlot, 3% Cabernet Franc, 3% Petit Verdot; aged 75% new French oak) and 2020 (60% CS, 31% M, 5% PV, 4% CF) were researched.
selection_notes: Use as left-bank Cabernet benchmark in cross-origin Cabernet flights against Napa or Coonawarra, or as the high point of a Medoc hierarchy flight against a Cru Bourgeois.

tasting_profile.appearance: Deep garnet to opaque blue-black with purple accents in youth. Wine Spectator (2020, No. 3 wine of 2023) notes the wine leads off with deep cassis and violet notes suggesting intense colour extraction. The Wine Cellar Insider describes '96-98... blue-black with purple accents.' Excellent depth and concentration visible in the glass.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Pure creme de cassis and blackcurrant dominate, with layered notes of graphite, cedar, lead pencil, sweet tobacco, iron, and violet. Wine Spectator (2020) describes 'cassis and violet notes leading off, followed by additional waves of black cherry and blackberry fruit as well as sweet tobacco and iron, with subtle savory and cedar hints.' The 2022 shows 'powerful blackberry and boysenberry fruit, cedar and light tobacco notes.' The 2021 Wine Spectator (94 pts) notes 'caressing black cherry and black currant paste flavours, with warm cast iron, sweet tobacco.' James Suckling (99 pts, 2020) highlights 'a beautifully layered red with blackcurrant, hazelnut and lead-pencil character.'
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Full-bodied, rich, concentrated, and powerful yet with remarkable purity. The 2020 (Wine Spectator No. 3, 98 pts) is described as offering 'almost approachable fruit' with an 'iron note piercing through the fruit on the finish.' The Wine Cellar Insider notes the wine is 'rich and fat' with 'a refined, stern, masculine quality to the fruit with a finish that lasts and sustains the layers of fresh, sweet black currants.' Multiple vintages show the house style of power with polish -- Lynch-Bages consistently 'performs at a second-growth level' per Robert Parker. Cassis, graphite, and cedar persist through a very long finish.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Acid: medium-plus, providing freshness and structure. Body: full. Alcohol: 13-14.1% depending on vintage. Tannin: firm and fine-grained to velvety, with structural backbone for decades of ageing. Finish: very long (50+ seconds in top vintages), with persistent cassis, cedar, and iron. Oak: 18 months French oak, approximately 75% new -- significant but well-integrated, contributing graphite, cedar, and toast complexity.
sources: Wine Spectator / James Molesworth (98 pts, 2020 vintage -- No. 3 Wine of 2023)
The Wine Cellar Insider (96-98 pts, 2019 en primeur)
Wine Spectator (2022 en primeur tasting note)

vintage_guidance: Use recent vintage; 2019 (74% Merlot, 26% Cabernet Franc; 18 months French oak, 50% new) and 2022 were researched. Estate composition is roughly 70% Merlot / 30% Cabernet Franc on the limestone plateau.
selection_notes: Right-bank Merlot-led counterpoint to Medoc Cabernet for Bordeaux blend-logic and bank discrimination questions; limestone chalkiness is the diagnostic regional marker.

tasting_profile.appearance: Deep ruby with intense purple/violet hue and black core in youth. Tastingbook (2020) notes 'intense purple colour with violet hue and black core.' Jeb Dunnuck (2020) describes 'a deep ruby hue.' The Wine Cellar Insider notes Canon 2022 shows exceptional colour depth. Brilliant clarity with good legs reflecting 13.5-14.5% ABV.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Extraordinarily pure and perfumed nose of red and black fruits (black raspberries, wild strawberries, darker cherries), pressed violets, iris, and spring flowers. Pronounced chalky minerality, crushed stones, and graphite -- the limestone plateau signature. Jeb Dunnuck (2022, 100 pts) notes 'ripe black cherries, graphite, crushed stone, and a beautiful floral character.' Wine Advocate (2020, 100 pts) describes 'aromas of cherries and raspberries mingled with notions of exotic spices and iris.' Neal Martin (Wine Advocate, 2019, 98 pts) highlights 'black raspberries, wild strawberries, spring flowers, chalky minerality, and graphite.' The Wine Cellar Insider adds 'mint, crushed stones, licorice, and spice.'
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Medium to full-bodied with seamless elegance, perfectly polished chalky tannins, and vibrant acids. The defining character is extraordinary purity and finesse rather than power. Wine Advocate (2020, 100 pts) describes it as 'deep and concentrated, with vibrant acids and beautifully polished, chalky tannins. Pure and precise, it concludes with a long, mouthwatering finish.' Vinous (2020, 98 pts) notes 'medium-bodied with fine tannins, fresh and almost citric, very vibrant and precise with a quite peppery finish.' The Wine Cellar Insider emphasises the 'polished silk profile' with 'layers of sensuous fruits, freshness and purity, finishing with a touch of salt.'
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Acid: medium-plus to high, vibrant, providing the essential freshness and limestone-driven energy. Body: medium to full. Alcohol: 13.5-14.5% depending on vintage. Tannin: ultra-fine, chalky, perfectly integrated -- the limestone plateau hallmark. Finish: very long to exceptionally long (50-60+ seconds in top vintages), with persistent mineral, salt, and pure red fruit. Oak: 18 months French oak, circa 50% new -- seamlessly integrated, never dominant.
sources: Jeb Dunnuck (100 pts, 2022 vintage -- No. 60 in Top 100 of 2025)
William Kelley, Wine Advocate (100 pts, 2020 vintage)
Neal Martin, Wine Advocate (98 pts, 2019 vintage)
The Wine Cellar Insider (98-99 pts across 2019-2022 vintages)

wine_name: Chateau Smith Haut Lafitte Rouge Grand Cru Classe
vintage_guidance: Use recent vintage; 2016 (65% Cabernet Sauvignon, 30% Merlot, 4% Cabernet Franc, 1% Petit Verdot; 18 months oak, 60% new from in-house cooperage) and 2022 (64% CS, 32% M, 3% CF, 1% PV) were researched.
selection_notes: Adds a Graves-style Cabernet-led blend variation against Medoc Pauillac for sub-regional discrimination on the left bank; smoky/graphite Graves character is the diagnostic shift versus Pauillac cassis-and-cedar.

tasting_profile.appearance: Deep garnet-purple to dark red, almost black in youth. Lisa Perrotti-Brown (2016) notes 'a deep garnet color'; Wine Advocate (2019) describes 'deep garnet-purple in color.' Millesima (2016) states 'a dark red color, almost black.' Opaque core with purple rim in youth. Excellent concentration visible in the glass.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Intense and multi-layered nose led by blackcurrant (cassis), blackberry, and dark plum, with the diagnostic Graves smoky/graphite character distinguishing it from Medoc. James Suckling (2018, 98 pts) notes 'plum and cherry aromas, wet earth and spice, very floral and vivid.' Lisa Perrotti-Brown (2016, 99 pts) finds 'dramatic notes of creme de cassis, blueberry compote, and chocolate-covered cherries, plus star anise, lavender, and fallen leaves, with a touch of iron ore.' Wine Advocate (2019) describes 'blackcurrant preserves, stewed plums, juicy blackberries, candied violets, licorice, espresso, tapenade, crushed rocks and wood smoke.' The smoky/flinty mineral note is a recurrent Graves terroir marker across vintages.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Full-bodied, rich, and opulent with polished, refined tannins and remarkable textural elegance. Wine Spectator describes the house style as 'a caressing wave of cassis, plum puree and blackberry compote flavours with enticing savoury, licorice, warm loam and mineral accents, with a cashmere finish.' Wine Advocate (2019) notes 'very firm, super-ripe, fine-grained tannins and bold freshness to support the voluptuous, mineral-accented fruit, finishing very long and achingly perfumed.' James Suckling (2018) praises 'layers of ripe tannins that are balanced and polished, incredibly polished and refined -- goes on for minutes.' The K&L (2021) note highlights 'velvety and seamless, deep and impressively multidimensional.'
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Acid: medium-plus, providing freshness and energy -- 'a certain sinewy, mineral quality that is quintessential Graves' (Jancis Robinson). Body: medium-full to full. Alcohol: 13.5-14.5%. Tannin: firm but fine-grained, polished, often described as 'cashmere' or 'silky' -- substantial but elegant. Finish: very long, with persistent smoky graphite, mineral, and dark fruit character. Oak: 18 months French oak (from in-house cooperage), approximately 60% new -- significant but integrated, contributing toast, cedar, and spice complexity. Organic/biodynamic practices since the Cathiard era.
sources: Lisa Perrotti-Brown / Wine Independent (99 pts, 2016 vintage)
Wine Advocate / Lisa Perrotti-Brown (97+ pts, 2019 vintage)
Wine Spectator / James Molesworth (house style description)

vintage_guidance: Use recent vintage; 2019 (65% Merlot, 29% Cabernet Sauvignon, 3% Cabernet Franc, 3% Petit Verdot; 14.5% abv; 12 months ageing, 50% one-year-old French oak / 50% tank) was researched.
selection_notes: Value-tier Medoc to use as the low end of a Bordeaux quality ladder against Lynch-Bages, or to test whether candidates can place a Bordeaux blend at mainstream price level without classed-growth concentration.

tasting_profile.appearance: Medium ruby-purple core with ruby rim; moderate depth of colour; clear; medium viscosity with slow-forming legs indicating moderate alcohol (14.5%)
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Floral character with plum, berry and cassis aromas; tobacco leaf and baking spice notes; underlying earthiness with hints of dried cherry, cedar and warm gravel; some barnyard nuance with clean mineral undertone; moderate intensity
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Soft entry with plum and cherry fruit, cassis and currant flavours; underlying earthiness and cocoa; tobacco leaf accent present mid-palate; gentle complexity rather than power; round red and dark fruit profile with subtle spice
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Medium-bodied; medium, polished tannins that are fine-grained and unassertive; moderate acidity providing freshness; 14.5% abv; medium finish with gentle cassis persistence; approachable and forward in style; not built for extended ageing but holds well for 10-15 years in good vintages
sources: The Wine Cellar Insider (88 pts, 2018 vintage)
Independence Wine & Spirits (2015 Cru Bourgeois tasting)
Palm Bay Wines (importer tech sheet, 2019 vintage)

wine_name: Heitz Cellar Napa Valley Cabernet Sauvignon
vintage_guidance: Use recent release; 2018 (100% Cabernet Sauvignon, 13.8% abv; aged two years in a combination of large French oak casks and barriques, then one year in bottle before release) was researched.
sub_region: Napa Valley (multi-sub-AVA: Oakville, Rutherford, Howell Mountain, St. Helena, Oak Knoll, Calistoga)
selection_notes: Restrained, classically built Napa Cab useful in cross-origin Cabernet flights against Lynch-Bages or Wynns; lower alcohol and fresher profile than Caymus-style options, better-suited to variety identification questions where ripeness alone should not give away origin.

tasting_profile.appearance: Medium to deep ruby-garnet with clear rim; bright and vivid colour; good clarity; moderate viscosity consistent with restrained 13.8% abv
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Red cherry, currant and cranberry dominant with dark raspberry and spiced plum; savory edge with dried herbs including sage and bay leaf; hints of cassis, juniper and floral notes; subtle chalk and dust; restrained and more red-fruited than dark-fruited for a Napa Cabernet; some cedar and tobacco with age; touch of menthol and mint
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Fresh and lively with red cherry, currant and plum flavours; boysenberry and dried herb notes; chalk and mineral undertones; savory and bright rather than jammy; focused and nuanced with lovely energy; hints of dark chocolate and mint on the finish; classic Napa expression without overripeness
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Medium to full-bodied but lighter-styled for Napa Valley; fine-grained tannins that are well-integrated and lightly drying; bright acidity providing freshness and lift; 13.8% abv (notably low for modern Napa); medium-plus to long finish with light red-fruited tartness; two years in French oak barrels (combination large and small) plus one year bottle age before release
sources: The Napa Wine Project (detailed 2021 and 2015 tasting notes)
Wine Spectator (2021 review: full-bodied, textural, rounded mouthfeel)

vintage_guidance: Use recent vintage; 2019 (100% Cabernet Sauvignon, 13.8% abv, 14 months in new (31%) and seasoned French hogsheads (61%), barriques (26%) and vats (13%)) and 2022 (96-97 pt) were researched.
selection_notes: Coonawarra's benchmark Cab; pairs against Napa Cab and Bordeaux left-bank Cab for same-variety cross-origin flights. Mint/eucalypt and terra rossa-driven freshness are the diagnostic regional markers.

tasting_profile.appearance: Deep ruby to opaque purple core with vibrant plum hue; excellent depth of colour; clear and bright; moderate viscosity
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Classic Coonawarra nose of black fruits with hint of mint and eucalyptus; blackberry, black cherry, cassis and dark cherry aromas; lavender and violet notes; savouriness with dried herbs, aniseed and white sage; subtle chocolate and black jellybean characters; oak embrace that adds rather than intrudes; slight dusty, chalky quality
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Dark cherry and blackberry pastille flavours with cassis of Coonawarra cabernet; seductive cherry and red berry augmenting the darker fruit; high energy and brightness driving to an effortlessly long finish; fine cedary oak; notes of moss and earth providing contrast; thyme on the finish; highly polished and elegant with suave poise
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Medium-bodied (described consistently as medium-weight); very fine, elegant tannins that are chalky-textured and pliable; bright acidity assisting length and lingering beautifully; 13.5-14% abv; long to very long finish; pristine varietal cabernet fruit balanced for cellaring (20+ years potential); matured 14 months in French oak hogsheads and barriques (21-31% new)
sources: Wynns Coonawarra Estate (official 2018 and 2023 tasting notes)
Halliday Wine Companion / Tastingbook (2023 vintage, 94 pts)

vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2023/2024 widely available. 2017 cited for detailed analytical (47.2 g/L RS, 9.6 g/L TA, 8% abv); 2024 is regarded as a classic Kabinett year.
selection_notes: Benchmark Mosel Kabinett: ~8% abv, 35-45 g/L RS, racy slate-driven acid. Use as the off-dry / fruity Mosel anchor in any Riesling sweetness ladder (Kabinett -> Spätlese -> Auslese) or in a cross-country dry-vs-off-dry Riesling spread (vs Alsace Frédéric Émile or Clare Polish Hill). ID deliberately distinct from existing Loosen Sekt entry.

tasting_profile.appearance: Pale straw to light lemon-green; brilliant clarity with green-gold tints; light viscosity with fine, quick legs consistent with low alcohol (8% abv); possible micro-bubbles from residual CO2
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Delicate and refined with white peach, lemon zest and Meyer lemon tea aromas; lavender and honeydew melon; wet stone and slate minerality; herbal and spicy notes including hibiscus, blood orange and rosemary in some vintages; hints of passion fruit and mandarin orange; also showing green apple (Granny Smith) and yellow plum; shy but elegant
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Lively and dancing on the palate with white peach, lemon zest and ginger; passion fruit, saline and quince notes; pineapple and citrus nuances; beautifully light and vividly citric; delicately sweet with classic Mosel balance of fruit and racy acidity; pure peach and lemon fruit lifted by crisp blue-slate minerality
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Light-bodied; high, racy acidity from blue Devonian slate soils; delicately off-dry with ~47 g/L RS balanced by ~9.6 g/L TA; 8% abv; medium-plus finish that is delicately sweet with sustained acidity; brisk and mouthwatering mouthfeel; quintessential Mosel Kabinett elegance; ungrafted vines averaging 60 years old
sources: Wine Enthusiast (2022 review: cooked apple, white currant, pineapple)
Ken's Wine Guide (91 pts, 2022 vintage, detailed tasting)
Dr. Loosen / Tastingbook (producer description and vintage notes)

vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2023 and 2024 widely circulating. JJ Prüm publishes no formal tech sheets, only vintage reports — research the specific vintage via the estate's vintage report and importer notes.
rs_level: sweet (~60-90 g/L typical Prüm Spätlese band; not formally disclosed by estate)
selection_notes: Iconic single-vineyard Spätlese from Mosel's most celebrated producer. Deep grey weathered Devonian slate, 70% slope, SSW exposure, 5 ha estate holding. Use as the upper rung of a Mosel sweetness ladder (Kabinett -> Spätlese -> Auslese) or as a sugar-acid-balance trap question — looks sweet on the nose, but acid + alcohol balance hides RS. Pairs with Loosen Kabinett to create a single-village, single-variety, ascending Prädikat flight.

tasting_profile.appearance: Medium lemon-yellow with obvious greenish tint; brilliant clarity; vibrant depth of colour from late-harvest concentration; light viscosity consistent with low alcohol (8% abv); notable brightness and vibrancy
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Pure, clear and flinty; concentrated fresh fruit salad quality served on a slate plate; apricot and white peach with officinal herbs (marjoram dominant); smoky, reductive 'sponti' character from indigenous yeast fermentation that evolves with bottle age; honey and apricot in older examples; wildness that develops into extraordinary complexity; silky succulence
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Super-delicate and precise despite considerable concentration; savory, clear and elegant with great precision and finesse; compact and tasty with perfect astringency and great minerality; lush and highly finessed but seriously structured; barely medium-bodied yet enormously long finish where natural grape sweetness is barely felt; citrusy persistence; stimulating salinity and mouth-watering grip
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Light to barely medium-bodied; high, penetrating acidity providing serious structure and stimulating salinity; off-dry to medium-sweet (Spatlese level RS) but perceived as barely sweet due to immense acidity; 8% abv; enormously long finish; aged in traditional large-format old oak Fuder casks on lees; 70% ungrafted vines averaging 60 years, some exceeding 100 years; legendary ageability (drinking windows extending 25+ years)
sources: Wine Advocate (95+ pts, 2020 vintage: 'pure, clear and flinty')
James Suckling (96 pts, 2020 vintage: 'beams you up to Cloud 9')
Tastingbook (99 Tb points, 2019 vintage: 'apricot, white peach, marjoram')
WineBerserkers forum (2019 range tasting notes from Katharina Prum event)
YouTube wine review (2016 magnum: detailed colour, nose, palate assessment)

vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; producer PDF expertise sheets are published per vintage. Recent vintages 2021, 2022, 2023 widely available.
classification: VDP.Grosse Lage — Grosses Gewächs (dry Erste Lage equivalent)
selection_notes: Iconic single-vineyard dry Rheingau Riesling from steep SW-facing phyllite/loess slope. Use as the dry German Riesling anchor against off-dry Mosel Kabinett (same variety, opposite RS) or against Trimbach Frédéric Émile (dry Riesling cross-country, Germany vs France) or Grosset Polish Hill (Old World vs New World dry Riesling). Highlights the VDP GG quality tier vs Prädikat ladder distinction.

tasting_profile.appearance: Medium straw-gold; brilliant clarity; moderate viscosity reflecting fuller body and higher alcohol for a dry German Riesling; slight green-gold tints
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Complex and enticing with ripe apple, juicy pear and citrus fruits; nectarine, quince and elderflower; tangerine and yellow plum with citrus peel; honey and green apple; white peach, pomelo and touch of petrol; dense and reductive with sesame and corn flake notes in youth; fine mineral notes of slate and flint; subtle wild hops
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Juicy and almost bouncy with Reine Claude (greengage) vividness; zesty ripe lemon resting on gentle yeast layer; concentrated with glimpses of tropical fruit within a frame of citric freshness; lime juice and green apple on the palate; cool and dynamic; vivid structure with brilliant citrus and orchard fruit; wet stone and saline nuance; creamy acidity with savory length
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Medium to full-bodied (described as a 'powerhouse' and 'muscular elegance'); vibrant, laser-focused acidity providing tension and linearity; bone-dry (Trocken); approximately 12.5-13% abv; very long, clean finish echoing lime zest, wet stone and mineral; aged 10-12 months on lees in large neutral oak Doppelstuckfasser (Stockinger casks); phyllite soils with loess and loam; vines up to 80 years old, many ungrafted; exceptional ageing potential
sources: Vinous / Anne Krebiehl MW (96 pts, 2022 vintage: detailed tasting)
Wine Enthusiast (2021 vintage: nectarine, quince, elderflower, wild hops)
CellarTracker community (2021 vintage: white fruit, cool, spicy, bright acidity)
Bibendum Wine Co / Decantalo (producer notes and multiple vintage reviews)

vintage_guidance: Released ~7 years post-vintage. 2015, 2017, 2018, 2019 widely circulating. 2017 importer tech sheet: 13.5% abv, RS 2.4 g/L, TA 7.64 g/L, pH 3.08.
classification: Blended from Grand Cru Geisberg and Grand Cru Osterberg sites (historically labelled Alsace AOC, not Grand Cru on the bottle)
rs_level: dry (2-3 g/L typical; 2.4 g/L on 2017 tech sheet)
selection_notes: Reference dry Alsace Riesling — higher abv (13.5%), bone-dry, no MLF, muschelkalk minerality and developed kerosene/smoke with age. Use as the Alsace anchor in a cross-country dry Riesling flight (Rheingau GG / Alsace FE / Clare Polish Hill / Eden Valley Pewsey Vale). Sets up the classic Alsace vs Mosel sweetness-and-alcohol contrast.

tasting_profile.appearance: Pale to medium straw-gold when young, developing to deep gold with age; brilliant clarity; moderate viscosity reflecting medium to full body; green-gold tints in youth
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Great aromatic complexity with citrus, white flowers and flinty mineral notes; green apple, pear, lime and orange zest; quince and honey developing with age; petrol (kerosene) note that dissipates; apricot, ripe pear; chalky, crushed stone and slight sea air quality; smoky and lemony; toasted bread and white truffle with significant age; vibrant mineral and racy
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Dry with halogen brightness; gorgeous acidity bringing to life flavours from fresh lemon juice and honey to paraffin and nut skin; pineapple and tropical hints balanced by lingering almond notes; stony and crisp with penetrating quality; peach, apple and chalk; slightly viscous texture; compact, dense and youthful even after years; saline and tensioned with complex finish pairing ripe dense fruit with structural elements
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Medium to full-bodied; high, chiselled acidity that is the defining structural feature; bone-dry (under 5 g/L RS in 2017; 2.4 g/L RS); 13-14% abv; very long to extraordinarily long finish; no MLF; vinified in stainless steel and old casks; grapes from Grand Crus Geisberg and Osterberg on marl-calcaro-sandstone soils; old vines 45-55+ years; released ~7 years post-vintage; ageing potential 20-30+ years; fine tannins reflecting limestone/marl terroir
sources: Robert Parker's Wine Advocate (95 pts, 2015 and 2019 vintages)
James Suckling (95 pts, 2017 vintage from magnum)
Vinous (95+ pts, 2017 vintage: green apple, pear, lime, mineral)
Wine Spectator (2017 vintage: spiced apple, pear jelly, quince, chalky mineral)
IntoWine.com (2001 and 2011 vintage reviews: peach, minerality, long finish)

vintage_guidance: Recent vintages 2022, 2023, 2024 widely circulating. 2019 producer tech sheet: 12.5% abv, RS 1.9 g/L, pH 2.97, TA 7.3 g/L. 2022 abv 12.9%; 2023 12.9%; 2024 12.1%.
classification: Langton's Classification: Exceptional. ACO-certified organic and biodynamic estate vineyard.
rs_level: bone_dry (1-2 g/L; 1.9 g/L on 2019 tech sheet)
selection_notes: Australia's iconic dry Riesling — bone-dry, screwcap, blue-slate-driven Polish Hill sub-region with very high acid and low pH. Use as the New World anchor in a global dry Riesling spread. Powerful contrast vs Mosel off-dry slate (same blue slate motif, opposite RS) and vs Rheingau GG (both dry, but Rheingau is fuller-bodied on phyllite vs Clare's lean structure). A classic 'Old vs New World same-variety' question piece.

tasting_profile.appearance: Pale lemon-green to pale straw when young; brilliant clarity; light viscosity; developing to deeper lemon and eventually old gold with extended age; characteristically pale and bright in youth
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Vivid lime and kaffir lime with lemon blossom; green apple and crushed slate minerality; slightly waxy and chalky notes overlaying the citrus; pungent yet restrained; grapefruit, gingery apple juice and jasmine tea aromas; white pepper and talc-chalk; bath salts and gunmetal character; spearmint and merest petrol note with age; lemongrass and subtle herbs
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Bone-dry and steely with intense lime and lemon driving the palate; grapefruit and quince emerging mid-palate; crystalline mineral quality with great intensity; piercing acid line with chalky, puckering texture; pink grapefruit and talc-chalk minerality; concentrated, fresh and bright; fleshy texture from small berries (unfined and unfiltered); pinpoint focus and deft poise
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Light to medium-bodied; high, steely acidity from broken blue slate soils (500 million year old); bone-dry (RS 1.9 g/L); 12.1-12.9% abv; long to very long finish with cleansing, moreish aftertaste; stainless steel fermented; lees contact; certified organic and biodynamic; vines planted 1996 at 460-480m elevation on hard rocky alluvial loam over clay over blue slate; exceptional ageing potential (15-20+ years)
sources: Huon Hooke / The Real Review (96 pts, 2025 vintage)
Halliday Wine Companion / Mike Bennie (95 pts, 2025 vintage)
Campbell Mattinson / The Wine Front (96 pts, 2025 vintage)
James Suckling (2025 vintage: lemon blossoms, kaffir limes, crushed slate)
Wine-pages.com / CellarTracker (multi-vintage tasting: spearmint, petrol, poise)

vintage_guidance: Recent vintages 2023, 2024, 2025 widely available. Importer tech sheet (2024): 11.5% abv, RS 1.20 g/L, TA 6.80 g/L, pH 3.1. Winemaker Louisa Rose.
classification: Sustainable Winegrowing Australia certified. First Riesling vineyard planted in Eden Valley (1847).
selection_notes: Mainstream-priced Eden Valley dry Riesling — historically the first Riesling vineyard planted in Eden Valley (1847). Lower abv (11.5%) and softer natural acidity than neighbouring Clare reflects Eden Valley's higher-elevation, cooler-but-rounder profile vs Clare's steely austerity. Use as the regional-tier Australian Riesling reference (vs Grosset Polish Hill iconic tier), or as the price/quality contrast in a Riesling sweep, or as a Clare-vs-Eden intra-Australian sub-region trap. Selected over Eroica/Henschke Julius because Pewsey Vale publishes a full importer tech sheet PDF with verified analytical numbers.

tasting_profile.appearance: Pale straw with green hues; brilliant clarity; light viscosity consistent with 11.5% abv; bright lemon-straw colour; green tints characteristic of cool-climate, high-altitude Riesling
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Intense lime and citrus with white flowers and fine dried herbs; fresh lemon and lemon blossom; slight floral aspect with delicacy; minerally bath salts character; touch of petrol developing with age; stone fruit and peach undertone; lemon rind edginess; not lean despite citrus dominance, with a sense of ripeness and burgeoning fruit
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Dry with intense lime streaking through the palate; grapefruit and lemon with fresh rosemary and white pepper; green apple crunch; blossom character carrying through from nose providing filigree elegance; hint of tropical fruit; minerally feel as mouth-watering acidity carries to a long refreshing finish; rich and vivacious yet uncompromisingly intense; lime and acid-shaped
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Light to medium-bodied; high, fresh natural acidity balancing flavour intensity (TA 6.80 g/L, pH 3.1); dry (RS 1.20 g/L); 11.5% abv; medium-plus to long finish marked by bright acidity, tart citrus and fine length; wild yeast fermentation since 2011; single vineyard at 500m elevation in Eden Valley; winemaker Louisa Rose; Eden Valley's longer ripening period from cooler temperatures; medium to long-term cellaring potential (15-20 years)
sources: Winebow / importer (2025 tech sheet and tasting notes)
OzWineReview (2024 vintage: steely, green-fruited, rocky acidity)
Wine-pages.com / Tom Cannavan (2020 vintage video review)

vintage_guidance: Current/recent vintage; 2022-2023 widely available. 2023 reviewed as 93pt JS — fresh, precise, restrained pyrazine.
selection_notes: Classic biodynamic Sancerre estate blend from all three local terroirs (silex, caillottes, terres blanches). Useful as the limestone/marl-driven Sancerre anchor against a flint-driven Pouilly-Fumé like Dagueneau Silex, or against a NZ Marlborough Sauvignon for F1 same-variety origin spreads.

tasting_profile.appearance: Pale lemon with green-gold highlights; brilliant clarity; light, watery tears suggesting moderate alcohol and dry extract
tasting_profile.nose_summary: White peach, candied papaya, grapefruit zest and gunflint on the initial nose. Secondary notes of honeysuckle, sea spray, tarragon and subtle floral honeyed character. Restrained pyrazine expression -- more stone fruit and mineral than green/grassy. The silex-limestone-marl terroir blend delivers layered mineral complexity rather than overt varietal typicity.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Frank, full and mature entry with clean citrus-driven flavours of grapefruit and lemon. Flinty, stony mid-palate with piquant citrus fruitiness. Rounded but muscular texture -- not chiseled. Chalky, mineral-infused finish with saline persistence. Laser-sharp focus with good balance between ripe fruit and high acidity.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: High acidity (vibrant, mouthwatering); no tannin; medium-minus body; 13-14% ABV well-integrated; dry; finish is medium-plus with lingering saline minerality and citrus peel

vintage_guidance: Allocation-tier across recent vintages; 2019/2020/2022 critically lauded. Use current release; expect long aging window.
selection_notes: Iconic Saint-Andelain silex-soil Pouilly-Fumé from old vines fermented partly in new 500/600L oak — the world reference for flint-driven, oak-aged Sauvignon Blanc. Critical foil to Sancerre (silex vs caillottes/marl, oak vs tank) and to NZ Sauvignon (savoury and structural vs fruit-driven).

tasting_profile.appearance: Medium to deep lemon-gold, approaching straw; brilliant clarity; moderate legs/tears suggesting 13-13.5% ABV and textural weight from barrel fermentation
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Intense gunflint, struck match and smoky minerality dominate the opening. Dried yellow plum, dried apricot, kiwi fruit and passion fruit emerge with air. Robust grapefruit with bitter pith, white pepper, and a salty saline suggestion. Subtle oak polish beneath -- hints of cedar, vanilla, honeysuckle and bread dough. A fascinating combination of mineral intensity, tropical lift and restrained oak complexity. Develops green apple, fennel and chalk notes with extended aeration.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Completely dry. Smooth, creamy textured entry with intense concentration. Kiwi fruit, passion fruit, stone fruit and apricot poking through the creamy core. Forceful acidity brings salty, mineral precision to the mid-palate. Bitter citrus (grapefruit) and white fruit (apple, plum) drive the palate. Subtle cedar and vanilla notes from oak are impeccably integrated. Long, mineral-laced finish with saline persistence. Demands decanting -- opens dramatically over 2+ hours.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: High acidity (crisp, salivating, masked by fruit concentration); no tannin; medium body with notable textural weight from lees and barrel work; 13-13.5% ABV; bone dry; very long finish with building mineral intensity

vintage_guidance: 2022/2023 currently in market; 2023 rated JD 96, Wine Spectator 93. Long-aging wine.
selection_notes: Reference dry Vouvray from a single Première Côte vineyard (Le Mont, green-tinged clay over tuffeau). Defines the steely, flinty, age-worthy dry Chenin style — direct foil to Savennières (harder schist/volcanic terroir) and to SA Chenin (riper fruit profile). High cooldown weight because Huet/Vouvray/Foreau dominate Loire Chenin teaching examples.

tasting_profile.appearance: Pale gold to light golden with silver-green highlights in youth; brilliant clarity; light legs consistent with 13% ABV dry style
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Taut and precise: citrus (lemon, green apple), quince and white peach layered over smoky, flinty minerality. Crushed shell and saline notes form a mineral backbone. With age, develops honeyed notes, nuts, lanolin, pear and dried fruit character. The Le Mont site brings a reticent, flinty character that develops the strongest perfume with bottle age -- initially less charming than other Huet cuvees but ultimately the most complex.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Taut and dry with crystalline purity. Peach, quince and orange peel with underlying mineral depth. Medium-bodied with a slightly viscous, smooth texture despite the linearity. Clean saline finish with lingering citrus peel and wet stone. Well-balanced acidity that retains freshness even at elevated ripeness levels. Stops the drinker in their tracks with depth and concentration. Light-bodied yet concentrated in warm vintages (2023); steely and flinty in cooler years.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: High acidity (malic acid dominant -- no MLF encouraged, contributing to age-worthiness); no tannin; medium-minus to medium body; 13% ABV; dry (though residual sugar can vary slightly by vintage); long clean finish with mineral persistence; 10-20 year aging potential

vintage_guidance: Current release typically 2020-2022; 2022 widely listed. Long aging potential; built for cellar.
classification: Clos du Papillon — historic coulée, near-monopole (shared with Baumard)
selection_notes: Benchmark Savennières from one of the three grand-cru-level coulées (Clos du Papillon, with La Coulée de Serrant and La Roche aux Moines). Schist/volcanic soils give a more savoury, structured Chenin than Vouvray — ideal F2 contrast inside Loire Chenin and useful as the high-intensity Old World Chenin in same-variety international flights.

tasting_profile.appearance: Slightly golden-tinged to deep gold in colour (deeper than Vouvray); brilliant clarity; moderate viscosity suggesting richer body and higher extract from late-harvest fruit and extended lees ageing
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Beeswax, roasted almonds, golden apple and mirabelle plum with apricot and quince. Lifted by acacia honey and floral notes. Herbal, minerally complexity with savoury intensity -- tight and firm. Flinty, chalky notes with green apple and orange rind. Waxy, straw-like character typical of Savennieres Chenin. Creamy oak undertone from 420L demi-muids. Pronounced schist/volcanic mineral signature distinct from Vouvray's tuffeau.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Dry, mid-weight and intensely structured. Full-bodied with generous richness and powerful slate-driven minerality on the finish. Firm lemony acidity frames the concentrated palate. Mealy, herby, waxy flavours with quince, apricot and a distinguished touch of bitterness or toasted almonds on the finish. The long lees ageing gives a smooth, rounded texture, but the schist terroir maintains savoury tension and taut structure. More imposing and savoury than Vouvray -- classic Savennieres intensity.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: High acidity (racy, bracing -- Chenin's natural high acid preserved through biodynamic farming and earlier picking); no tannin; medium-plus body; 13.5-14% ABV (can reach 14.5-15% in riper vintages); dry; very long finish with incredible length and slate mineral persistence; 15-25 year aging potential

vintage_guidance: 2023 (14% ABV) and 2024 (13.5%, RS 1.8 g/l, TA 6.1 g/l, pH 3.22) currently in market. Distinct from the Mullineux Old Vines White blend already in the bank.
selection_notes: Different cuvée from the already-banked Mullineux Old Vines White (which is a blend with Clairette/Sémillon/Viognier/Verdelho); this is a single-vineyard, single-variety Chenin from 47-year-old dry-farmed bush vines on decomposed granite, fermented in neutral French oak. Cleanest F1 'Loire vs SA Chenin' foil — flintier and more linear than Iron/Schist cuvées, so it pairs closely against Vouvray Sec stylistically.

tasting_profile.appearance: Pale straw to light gold; brilliant clarity; light to moderate tears consistent with 13.5% ABV; slight oily texture visible on the glass
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Restrained and mineral-driven: real mineral saline note over apple, pear and slight citric lemon. Toasty, gunpowdery/gunflint notes from indigenous yeast fermentation (not new oak). Bright fruit flavours -- green apple, pear skin, crushed stone and Meyer lemon. Citrus blossom emerges with air. Stone fruit undertones. The granite terroir delivers a distinctly flinty, aromatic character -- more aromatic than the Schist or Iron cuvees due to fruit ripening in a cooler micro-environment.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Absolutely bone dry with mouthwatering acidity. Very precise with a taut, linear delivery. Medium to full-bodied with delightful mineral tension. Dusty pears, yellow and green apples, fresh-cut Meyer lemon. Sapid saline note on the finish with good length. Slight oily texture adds roundedness to the linear frame. Toasty complexity from indigenous fermentation, not oak. Cerebral wine that keeps you thinking -- gliding to a fresh, mineral-laced finish. Food-friendly for 20+ years.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: High acidity (striking vertical freshness -- highest natural acidity of the three Mullineux Chenin cuvees due to deep-rooted vines in granite); no tannin; medium to full body; 13.5% ABV; bone dry (RS 1.8 g/l in 2024); long finish with salty mineral persistence; 20+ year aging potential

vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2019 (a celebrated classic Barolo vintage) was researched.
selection_notes: Benchmark village-level Barolo from a top traditionalist family producer; assembled from three high-altitude sites (Fossati, La Volta, Coste di Vergne) in Vergne. Use as the Nebbiolo anchor against Barbaresco for same-variety/different-zone flights, or against a single-cru Barolo for an origin quality-ladder question. Pale garnet, high acid + high gripping tannin, tar/rose/red cherry profile, neutral large-cask oak.

tasting_profile.appearance: Medium ruby to garnet with a lively, pigeon-blood core; classic pale Nebbiolo translucency; moderate tears consistent with 14% ABV
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Bright and expressive: fresh cherries, red currant, macerated strawberries and raspberry. Crushed dried flowers, rose petal, violet and hawthorn florals. Interlaced with mint, lavender, rosehip, rhubarb and licorice. Hints of red fig and orange peel. With age, develops earthy, dusty notes with a slight balsamic quality. Sweet spices emerge alongside crushed stone and granite minerality. Aromatic and perfumed -- varietal authenticity is a signature of this high-altitude cuvee.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Generous, layered and youthful entry. Vibrant red fruit core -- marasca cherry, raspberry and red currant underscored by vibrant acidity. Harmonious and supple in texture, with peppery strawberry and crushed allspice notes. Iron and juniper give savoury complexity. Ripe but refined tannins frame a silky, multi-layered mid-palate. Classic finish on tones of marasca cherry, mineral and floral elements. Already irresistible in good vintages but built for moderate bottle ageing.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: High acidity (vibrant, energetic); high tannin (fine, velvet, ripe -- gripping but not aggressive); medium body; 14% ABV; dry; long aftertaste of red fruit, mineral and floral; best from release through 20+ years; 18-26 months in large Slavonian oak casks (neutral)

vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2022 is the current release researched.
selection_notes: The single most consistent benchmark of value Nebbiolo: a co-op assembled from ~50 grower-members and 120 ha across the Barbaresco DOCG, with 24 days on skins and ~24 months in large botti. Use as the natural Barbaresco partner against a village Barolo for a same-variety/cross-zone flight (Barolo more powerful and tannic, Barbaresco more perfumed and earlier-drinking), or as the entry tier in a Produttori cru ladder.

tasting_profile.appearance: Pale garnet to medium ruby; classic Nebbiolo translucency with slight orange-tinged rim on older vintages; clean and bright; moderate tears
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Rose, cherry, strawberry and violet florals. White pepper, dried cranberry and raspberry with cassis undertones. Leather, smoke, tobacco and cedar add complexity. Lavender, fig, balsamic, dried leaves and wet stone. Classic tar-and-roses Barbaresco character. In warmer vintages (2018), darker fruit with truffle, licorice, grilled herb and raisin/dried apricot. More perfumed and immediately aromatic than Barolo -- the cooperative blend captures the essence of multiple Barbaresco terroirs.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Vibrant and well-defined. Rose, cherry and strawberry fruit core with white pepper and mineral flavours. Sleek and elegant with firm, dusty yet ripe tannins. Smoky and juicy mid-palate. Medium to full body with good concentration. Long savory finish with mineral earth and fennel/thyme notes. Balanced and approachable in youth yet structured for ageing. Linear finish that firms up with excellent length. Classic scents of tar and roses carry through to the palate.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: High acidity (vibrant, fresh); high tannin (firm, dusty, well-integrated -- slightly softer than Barolo); medium to full body; 14% ABV; dry; long linear finish with excellent length; 24+ months in large botti (2,500-5,000L); best from 5 years after vintage through 20+ years

vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2019 (an excellent Brunello vintage) was researched.
selection_notes: Reference-standard Brunello from one of Montalcino's largest historic estates (Franceschi family, est. 1890s), built on the oldest estate vines between 150–450 m. 100% Sangiovese, submerged-cap fermentation, long aging in large French casks. Use as the Tuscan Sangiovese pinnacle against Chianti Classico Riserva and Vino Nobile in a same-variety/cross-zone flight, or as the upper rung of a Tuscan Sangiovese quality ladder.

tasting_profile.appearance: Ruby red with good depth and youthful intensity; saturated and vibrant in the glass with no sign of evolution; good full ruby-red colour with darker concentration than many peers in warm vintages
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Dark, earthy and complex: crushed ashen stone, rosemary, cedar and exotic spice open the bouquet. Core of raspberry preserves and black cherry. Violet inner florals, leather, graphite and tobacco weave through. Dried fruit and autumnal forest floor, dark licorice, wet slate, cola and grilled rosemary on second nose. Luscious red fruit (red plum, pomegranate) with outstanding mineral lift. Structured and layered rather than immediately expressive -- rewards patience. Roses washed in mulberry, cinnamon-spiced pomegranate and jasmine/tea leaf notes in top vintages.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Warm and balanced with velvet-smooth tannins. Silky texture that flows across the palate, building in tension. Tart wild berries and orange hints give way to a sweet herbal thrust. Dense but weightless and sappy with distinctly juicy fruitiness. Energizing minerality to fine-grained fruit and floral flavours. Plum, cherry, iron, tobacco and underbrush flavours buoyed by a plump texture. Firm, crunchy tannins that are powdery and dry. Tremendous length with classic structure. The 15% ABV is felt but integrated.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: High acidity (vibrant, focusing the dense structure); high tannin (powdery, dry, crunchy -- firm and youthful, needing time); full body; 15% ABV (warm, noticeable but integrated); dry; very long finish with building noble tannins and mineral persistence; best from 5-7 years after vintage through 25+ years; aged in large French botti (30-50 hl)

vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2020 was researched.
selection_notes: Benchmark single-vineyard Chianti Classico Riserva from the high (400–420 m) Castelnuovo Berardenga zone on alberese-and-galestro soils. 100% Sangiovese, French-barrique aged 16–20 months. Use as the Chianti Classico anchor in a Tuscan Sangiovese same-variety flight (vs Brunello + Vino Nobile), or as the cru-Riserva rung above a straight Chianti Classico annata in a quality-ladder question. The 100% Sangiovese composition contrasts cleanly with Boscarelli Vino Nobile's traditional Sangiovese-plus-native blend.

tasting_profile.appearance: Ruby red with rich, intense tones; medium-deep with garnet highlights; classic Sangiovese translucency at the rim; clean and bright
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Spicy nose with floral notes (violet) and hints of wild berry -- both red (Marasca cherry, raspberry) and black (black currant). Mineral impressions with crushed limestone/flint signature. Dried herb, balsamic, eucalyptus and new leather accents. Light toasted notes from French barrique ageing. Earth, tobacco and white truffle with tilled earth complexity. Wild cherry and raspberry dominate in youth; develops secondary complexity of incense, dried leaves and menthol with age. The dusty minerality is the wine's special signature.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Spice and crisp fruit on entry with self-confident but supple tannins. Impressive structure and breed. Ripe Marasca cherry and plum with brown spice, exotic incense and a minty finish. Beautiful minerality with dusty, crushed limestone or flint notes. Taut and linear, with elegant delivery. Firm tannins frame the elements, imparting structure and complexity for the long haul. Terrific texture -- silky in warm years (2018), more structured and stubborn in classic vintages (2016). The 100% Sangiovese purity shows through with a sour cherry, dried herb and tobacco core.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: High acidity (fresh, balancing -- signature of the Castelnuovo Berardenga zone at 400-420m altitude); medium-plus tannin (firm but supple, from French barriques aged 16-20 months); medium-plus body; 14% ABV; dry; vigorous and taut finish with supporting acidity; best from 5-10 years after vintage through 20+ years; needs patience -- hits prime at 15-20 years

vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2020/2022 are current releases researched.
selection_notes: Traditional Vino Nobile from a benchmark 1962-founded estate at Cervognano. 85% Sangiovese (Prugnolo Gentile) with the historic blend of Colorino, Canaiolo, Mammolo — preserves the older Tuscan Sangiovese-plus-natives style that diverges from the 100%-Sangiovese Brunello and Felsina Rancia. Slavonian + French Allier botti aging 18–24 months. Use as the third Sangiovese reference in a Tuscan cross-zone flight (Brunello / Chianti Classico / Vino Nobile) where the candidate must distinguish appellations of the same variety, or as the breadth wine in a mixed Tuscan reds question.

tasting_profile.appearance: Medium ruby with garnet reflections developing with age; good clarity and brightness; medium viscosity with slow-forming legs indicating moderate alcohol and extract
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Complex and inviting aromatic profile of sour cherry, marasca cherry, ripe red fruits and redcurrant, with roses, violets, and sweet spices. Underpinned by tobacco, chocolate, licorice, and dried herb nuances from oak aging. A pinch of sweetness and floral precision; in youth can be somewhat closed and reserved
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Elegant and harmonious with silky, smoothly polished tannins. Cherry flavour at the core with spiciness and a subtle background sweetness. Dense yet juicy with pure, fresh fruit. Riper and fleshier than Chianti Classico on average, but more restrained than Brunello. Mineral and floral nuances emerge on the finish
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Acidity: high, fresh and lively. Tannins: medium-plus, silky to firm depending on age, well-polished. Body: medium-plus. Alcohol: 13.5-14%. Finish: very long and persistent with mineral-floral precision. Built for medium to long aging

vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2020/2021 researched.
sub_region: Mount Etna (northern slope, Randazzo/Castiglione di Sicilia)
selection_notes: Reference Etna Rosso from Marco de Grazia's pioneer estate on the volcano's northern slope (600–900 m, partly pre-phylloxera). 95–98% Nerello Mascalese + 2–5% Nerello Cappuccio, large-format French oak 16 months. Plays the F5 outlier role: pale, high-acid, savory volcanic red that frequently gets mistaken for Pinot Noir or Nebbiolo in blind exam contexts — a classic curveball wine, increasingly common in modern MW Paper 2 / Paper 3 questions. Use as the Sicily/Etna anchor in a 'mediterranean island reds' or 'identify the variety' flight.

tasting_profile.appearance: Pale to medium ruby, bright and translucent with notable clarity; lighter in hue than most southern Italian reds, often mistaken for Pinot Noir or Nebbiolo on appearance alone; low to medium viscosity reflecting moderate alcohol and elegance
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Bright cherry fruit, wild strawberry, and tart red berry aromas with pronounced floral notes of violets and roses. Distinctive volcanic smoke and graphite minerality, with hints of white pepper, dusty strawberry, and herbs. Earthy, mineral, and clean with remarkable purity. Some vintages show blood orange and cranberry notes
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Medium-bodied with vibrant, tart red fruit flavours including red cherry, blood orange, and wild strawberry. Salty mineral undertones and iron-graphite character from volcanic soils. Herbal notes and a sweet-and-sour tension. Silky to fine-grained texture with excellent purity and vivid expression. Can show a Barolo-like or Burgundian complexity with age
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Acidity: high, vibrant and driving. Tannins: fine and persistent, can saturate deeply in youth with bitter cranberry-cherry pit finish. Body: medium. Alcohol: 13-14.5%. Finish: long with lingering iron-mineral, herbal, and tart red berry notes. Ages gracefully despite apparent delicacy

wine_name: Côte-Rôtie Brune et Blonde de Guigal
vintage_guidance: Released annually with long élevage; 2019 widely available, 2020 in market. Signature classique négoce blend across Côte-Brune and Côte-Blonde lieux-dits.
selection_notes: Reference Northern Rhône Syrah at benchmark quality and négoce house style. The 4% Viognier co-ferment, long 38-month élevage and partial new oak make this the textbook Côte-Rôtie style — useful as the Old World anchor in any cross-country Syrah/Shiraz flight (against Barossa, McLaren Vale, Hawke's Bay, Stellenbosch) and as the Côte-Rôtie reference inside a Northern Rhône quality ladder (Crozes → Saint-Joseph → Côte-Rôtie/Hermitage).

tasting_profile.appearance: Dark ruby red to inky purple in colour, opaque to mostly opaque with bright rim; high viscosity with pronounced legs reflecting 14% alcohol and extract from long oak aging
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Classic Cote-Rotie profile of red and black raspberries, boysenberry, and blackberry with ground black pepper, violet, candied flowers, and spice. Smoky, gently herbal nuances with hints of licorice, olive paste, smoked/cured meat, pencil shavings, and subtle oak-derived vanilla. Some vintages show cassis, black olive, earth, and dark chocolate. The Viognier co-ferment adds a floral lift and aromatic complexity
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Medium to full-bodied with supple, round tannins softened by long oak aging. Juicy red and blue fruit preserves, raspberry, blackberry, and vanilla core with spicecake, olive paste, and violet pastille flavours. Balanced and satisfying with excellent depth. Concentrated yet not heavy, with underlying char and savoury earthiness. Some vintages show a leaner, more elegant profile while others are richer and more concentrated
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Acidity: medium-plus, well-integrated and scarcely perceptible. Tannins: medium to medium-plus, round and velvety with slow-building grip from 38 months in 50% new French oak. Body: medium to full. Alcohol: 13-14%. Finish: very long, silky, and persistent with lingering floral, spice and savoury notes. Excellent aging potential of 10-20+ years

vintage_guidance: 2020 and 2021 currently in market; both researched. Estate organic since 1950s and biodynamic since 1974, on 100 ha within the appellation at the northern limit exposed to the Mistral.
selection_notes: Iconic Châteauneuf-du-Pape with the appellation's most Mourvèdre-led top-tier house style (regularly ~30% Mourvèdre vs Grenache-led peers). Anchors any Southern Rhône / GSM-style flight as the savoury, garrigue, structured benchmark to contrast against more Grenache-fruit-forward CdPs (Vieux Télégraphe, Pegau, Clos des Papes), Priorat blends, McLaren Vale GSM and Bandol. Long-aging foudre regimen rather than barrique gives a non-oaky structural profile useful for testing candidates on Mediterranean-blend recognition.

tasting_profile.appearance: Deep ruby-red to ruby-purple, with carmine highlights in youth; excellent clarity despite no fining; medium-plus viscosity reflecting full body and 14.5% alcohol. Shiny and bright
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Powerful, complex, and elegant with superb aromatic depth. Classic Beaucastel signature of dark wild berries, cassis, cherry puree, kirsch, and plum reduction interwoven with garrigue, dried herbs, smoked meat, black pepper, and leather. Secondary notes of cocoa, chocolate, tobacco, black licorice, roasted herbs, potpourri, and warm stone. The Mourvedre dominance gives a distinctive savoury, meaty, peppery character that differentiates it from Grenache-led CdPs. Violet and floral top notes from the diverse 13-variety assemblage
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Full-bodied and silky with dense, perfectly balanced structure. Generous and seamless with palate-staining blackcurrant, cherry-cola, and sweet red fruit flavours. Spicecake, leather, alder, and dried garrigue build with aeration. The Mourvedre contributes mouthcoating, deep tannins with plenty of flesh. Shows remarkable purity and clarity despite power. Energetic and impressively long with a sleek iron spine. Elegant texture with sweet tannins and great complexity
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Acidity: medium-plus, lively and fresh. Tannins: ripe, fine-grained, and structured; chewy in youth but velvety with age from foudre aging (no new oak). Body: full. Alcohol: 14.5%. Finish: very long and harmonious with lingering warm stone, spice, and sweet dark berry notes. Exceptional aging potential of 20+ years. Built to last

vintage_guidance: Annual release; current vintage in market each spring. World's reference Provence rosé and #1-selling rosé in the US; LVMH Wines & Spirits since 2019.
selection_notes: Category-defining Provence rosé — pale, dry, red-berry-and-citrus driven, stainless-steel-only, Grenache–Cinsault–Rolle led with Syrah/Tibouren accents. The MW Paper 3 anchor for any 'identify the rosé' question and the global commercial benchmark to contrast against saignée Tavel (deeper, GSM-heavy), Spanish rosado (Garnacha-led, fruitier), Loire Cabernet Franc rosé (red-fruit & herbal), and New World rosé. Use as the universal Provence rosé reference in a P3 mixed-style flight.

tasting_profile.appearance: Very pale salmon-pink to pale golden pink, almost between a white wine and a rose in colour; brilliant clarity; translucent with notable legs indicating moderate alcohol (13%). The benchmark for pale Provence rose colour
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Fresh, fruity, and immediately appealing with strawberry, raspberry, red berry, and subtle citrus (lemon zest, pink grapefruit, lime). Delicate peach, white cherry, and watermelon rind nuances. Floral notes of peony and white flowers. Hints of salty minerality and a touch of herbal character. Clean and aromatic without excessive intensity; more perfumed than pungent
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Sleek, dry, and crisp with mellow citrus and young strawberry flavours at the core. Red berry fruit from old-vine Grenache with subtle lees-derived creaminess and silky mouthfeel. Plump yet balanced with succulent lemony acidity. Light-bodied and refreshing with a seamless, soothing character. Tart pink grapefruit and subtle minerality add interest. Accessible and immediately enjoyable
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Acidity: medium-plus, crisp and fresh. Tannins: negligible. Body: light. Alcohol: 13%. Finish: medium, dry and lip-smacking with lingering fruit and a hint of salty minerality. Stainless steel only, no oak influence. Designed for immediate drinking within 1-2 years of vintage

vintage_guidance: 2021/2022 in market. Cuvée was renamed 'Lulu & Lucien' from the 2021 vintage onward in homage to founders Lucie (Lulu) and Lucien Peyraud, but the producer English page still references the 'Cuvée Classique' identity. Estate practising organic, biodynamic-influenced.
selection_notes: Reference Bandol and the global benchmark for Mourvèdre-led Mediterranean reds. ~75% Mourvèdre, foudre-aged with no new oak, framed by sea-influenced limestone clay terroir — savoury, gripping, herbal-iodine, long-lived. Use as the Mourvèdre anchor in any 'identify the variety' P2 / P3 flight against Monastrell from Jumilla/Yecla (riper, plumper), Mataró-led California, and against other Provence reds (Bandol's iodine-leather signature is the discriminator). Pairs with Beaucastel CdP in a Mourvèdre-aware Southern French blend flight.

tasting_profile.appearance: Deep ruby-red to dark ruby with good concentration; can appear deceptively light on pour before revealing depth; medium-plus viscosity. Bright and clear despite no fining or filtration
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Rich and complex with dark berry fruit (blackberry, boysenberry, black cherry) layered with Mourvedre-typical reduction that can present as smoky, earthy, and feral in youth. Tobacco, old leather, wet autumn leaves, iron, and worn leather. Garrigue, dried herbs, violet, and spice box notes. A distinctive iodine-saline character from Bandol's maritime terroir. Can show a seductive prettiness with violet and graphite shavings alongside crunchy red plum. The Mourvedre funk evolves and integrates with bottle age
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Robust yet refined with layers of black cherry, dark cherry, plum, and boysenberry. The Grenache adds sweetness while Cinsault and Carignan provide rustic complexity. Earthy Mourvedre undertones with soy, iron, and leather flavours. Medium-weight and broad in good vintages, with a silk to grippy texture. Salt and bitterness on the finish in youth that resolves with age. Dark, dense, and powerful with a distinctive Bandol iodine-leather signature that distinguishes it from all other Mourvedre-based wines
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Acidity: medium-plus. Tannins: firm and grippy in youth, dense and structured; can be tannic and brooding when young, requiring significant age to soften. Body: full. Alcohol: 14-14.5%. Finish: long and satisfying with hints of spice, mineral, and lingering dark fruit. Best with 8-12+ years of age; the Classique is the most approachable of the Tempier range but still built for the long haul. No new oak (foudre-aged 18-20 months)

vintage_guidance: Released annually; 2022/2023 in market. Foillard is a charter member of the 'Gang of Four' Beaujolais natural-wine pioneers (with Lapierre, Thévenet, Breton) under Jules Chauvet's tutelage; flagship cuvée from the famed Côte du Py decomposed-granite/schist hill of Villié-Morgon.
selection_notes: Reference Cru Beaujolais and the textbook 'serious Gamay' style — whole-cluster, semi-carbonic, aged in neutral Burgundy barrels, no chaptalization or filtration. Foillard's Côte du Py is the canonical Pinot-Noir-adjacent red used in MW flights to test whether candidates over-jump to Burgundy: the iron-granite signature, brisker acid, and lighter tannin profile (vs Pinot's silkier tannin and cherry-stone earth) is the discriminator. Anchors the cru tier against village Beaujolais-Villages and basic Beaujolais in a quality-ladder flight.

tasting_profile.appearance: Medium ruby to medium-deep ruby, bright and clear; lighter than most reds but deeper than village Beaujolais. Good translucency reflecting the Gamay grape and whole-cluster vinification. Moderate viscosity
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Wonderfully complex with exquisite delineation: wild berries, dark cherry, raspberry, and crushed stone. Violets and subtle floral notes with hints of ash, cracked black pepper, and light stony/granite minerality. Some farmyard character (from natural winemaking) alongside sweet-and-sour red and black cherry. Spicy notes and a mineral-laden backbone. Can show reduction in youth that blows off. No bubblegum or banana esters typical of commercial Beaujolais
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Deep, structured, and complex with a velvety lushness that makes it irresistible when young. Silky yet structured with juicy red and black fruit wrapped around a core of iron-granite minerality. Sapid and vivacious with filigree tannins and a silver thread of acidity. Plump and juicy dark cherry and raspberry fruit with savoury earth, sweet-and-sour tension, and wild herb complexity. Develops Burgundian complexity with time. Rustic structure beneath the elegant exterior
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Acidity: medium-plus, brisk and fresh with great balance. Tannins: fine to medium, well-structured but silky, not aggressive. Body: medium to medium-plus. Alcohol: 13-13.5%. Finish: long and elegant with lingering freshness, savory earth, cracked pepper, and mineral persistence. Ages beautifully for 5-15 years despite accessible youth. No new oak (neutral used Burgundy barrels, 6-9 months)

vintage_guidance: Annual release; current vintage in market. The category-defining mass-market Beaujolais-Villages — Duboeuf is nicknamed the 'King of Beaujolais' and is the world's largest commercial négociant-shipper of Beaujolais Nouveau and AOC Beaujolais. Sourced from 38 Villages-eligible communes via more than 300 contracted growers.
selection_notes: Commercial Beaujolais-Villages floor for the Beaujolais quality ladder — light-bodied, low-tannin, fruit-forward Gamay with the textbook semi-carbonic banana/strawberry ester profile that betrays Beaujolais's carbonic identity. Useful as the value foil against Cru Beaujolais (Foillard Morgon) and against Pinot Noir / Loire Gamay in a 'name the variety AND quality tier' question. Standard global négociant style — appears in supermarket and bistro lists worldwide.

tasting_profile.appearance: Brilliant garnet to purple-red, light in body but deeper in colour than expected; good clarity and brightness. Light viscosity with quickly falling tears reflecting lower alcohol and light extract
