tasting_profile.nose_summary: Textbook semi-carbonic Beaujolais character with incomparable fruitiness in the purest Gamay expression. Strawberry, raspberry, and banana aromas (strawberry-banana jello quality from carbonic maceration esters). Black fruits and creme de cassis with cherry and blueberry notes. Some floral character. Intensity is moderate and immediately fruit-forward. A prototypical Beaujolais-Villages aromatic profile with hints of pomegranate
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Juicy sweet berry fruit dominated by strawberry, red plum, and red cherry flavours. Round and vinous with soft, delicate tannins contributing to a smooth, silky texture. Light-bodied and very easy to drink. Simple fruit-forward character without complexity. Barely medium-bodied with supple tannins and generous fruit. Harmonious, moderately dry finish that keeps giving fruit. Confected berry quality with surprising structure for the price tier
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Acidity: medium, adequate but not driving. Tannins: low, very soft and smooth. Body: light to medium. Alcohol: 12.5-13%. Finish: medium-short to medium, fruity and clean. Harmonious and complete but without depth or complexity. Designed for immediate drinking within 1-2 years. Stainless steel, no oak

vintage_guidance: Non-vintage; recent disgorgements based on the 2019/2021 vintages with 25% reserve wine. Researched batch: NV "White Foil" disgorged October 2024.
selection_notes: Benchmark grande-marque NV — equal-thirds Pinot Noir/Meunier/Chardonnay, 25% reserve, 4 years on lees, 8–10 g/L dosage. Sits ABOVE the existing commercial-tier De Laurency NV foil in the bank and provides the proper NV-Champagne reference for any quality-ladder question (commercial NV → grande-marque NV → vintage prestige cuvée) and for cross-country traditional-method flights (vs Cava, Franciacorta, Nyetimber).

tasting_profile.appearance: Beautiful golden straw to pale gold with silver reflections; brilliant clarity. Abundant, fine, and persistent mousse with elegant bead. Creamy effervescence typical of long lees aging (4+ years)
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Powerful and attractive with aromas of ripe pear, yellow apple, mango, and cooked pip fruit (quince jelly, apricot). Light flavours of honeysuckle, white jasmine, and citrus blossom. Brioche, toast, vanilla, and beeswax notes from extended lees contact. Hints of mandarin oil, honeycomb, elderflower, fresh bread, and musky peach. Can show Amalfi lemon, rhubarb, and anise. Clear, precise, and fresh with both fruit and autolytic complexity
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Medium-bodied with a deliciously fine and creamy mousse. Round and fruit-driven with golden apple, honeycomb, nectarine, and poached pear flavours. Lemon zest, candied orange peel, and ginger nuances. Cooked fruit qualities (quince, apricot jam) mingle with beeswax and acacia honey. Charming and perfectly balanced with pleasant freshness and underlying structure. Fleshy and enveloping with a generous core of fruit, lively acids, and a pillowy mousse. Red-fruited undertones from the Pinot Noir component
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Acidity: high, dynamic and fresh. Tannins: n/a (sparkling). Body: medium. Alcohol: 12.5%. Mousse: fine, creamy, and persistent. Dosage: 8-10 g/L (Brut). Finish: long-lasting and aromatic with both fruity (candied citrus, tangerine) and spicy (cardamom, anise) notes; steely and mineral on the close. Elegant and refined with taut persistence. Equal thirds Pinot Noir/Meunier/Chardonnay with 25% reserve wines

vintage_guidance: Vintage prestige cuvée; only made in exceptional years. 2018 vintage researched (released 2025/2026). 2015 vintage also actively in market.
sub_region: Aÿ / Montagne de Reims / Côte des Blancs
classification: Vintage prestige cuvée — Grand Cru and Premier Cru parcels only
selection_notes: Iconic vintage Champagne, Pinot-dominant, vinified entirely in old oak barriques and aged ~7 years on cork — the textbook NV-vs-vintage and stainless-vs-oak Champagne contrast in any prestige-tier flight. 2018: 66% PN / 34% Chard from 19 crus, ~73% Grand Cru / 27% Premier Cru, 7 g/L dosage. 2015: 60/40 PN/Chard, 11 crus, 8 g/L. Use against Pol Roger NV and Krug-style competition or to anchor the upper rung of a Champagne quality ladder.

tasting_profile.appearance: Medium-deep golden hue with fine, persistent mousse and excellent bead definition. Bright clarity with legs suggesting medium-plus body. The colour is deeper gold than typical NV Champagne, reflecting Pinot Noir dominance and barrel fermentation in old oak.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Enticing fragrant aromas of toast, lemon zest and an earthy-smoky nuance typical of the Bollinger house style. Stone fruits including Mirabelle plum and pear are layered with honey, toasted hazelnuts, almond, and vanilla. Red fruit undertones of blackcurrant and raspberry emerge with air. Mocha and fino sherry-like oxidative hints from barrel fermentation. White peony florals on the 2018; mango and exotic fruit on the 2015.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Plump, generous and enticing on entry, with flavours of stewed quince, Mirabelle plum jam, and Bosc pear on the 2015. Tropical pineapple and papaya notes on the 2015 vintage. Creamy effervescence with a texture-driven, layered mouthfeel. Toasted walnut, honey, and a dash of bitter marmalade add complexity. The finish blends chocolate and citrus fruits, lengthened by chalky mineral extract and hints of schist.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: High acidity providing freshness and lift. Medium to full body — more muscular and layered than typical NV Champagne. Fine persistent mousse. 12.5% ABV. Dosage 7-8 g/L (Brut). Dense core of fruit framed by chalky structuring extract. Long, sapid finish. The 2015 shows an old-school generous Bollinger profile; the 2018 reveals citrus freshness and almond.
sources: Wine Advocate (Robert Parker) — 95+ points, 2015 vintage, March 2024
Champagne Bollinger official tasting notes (champagne-bollinger.com) — 2015 and 2018 vintages
Vintus (US importer) — technical notes for 2015 and 2018
JamesSuckling.com — feature article on 2015 LGA and LGA Rosé

vintage_guidance: Single-vineyard, single-vintage, brut nature. Vintage releases lag heavily due to ~150-month minimum lees ageing. Current release: 2012 (ca. 1,561 numbered bottles). 2009/2011 also in market via verticals.
appellation: Corpinnat (formerly Cava de Paraje Calificado prior to 2019)
classification: Single-vineyard sparkling wine, brut nature, 0.97 ha estate plot planted 1940
selection_notes: Spain's flagship terroir-driven traditional-method sparkling wine and the original Cava de Paraje Calificado before Recaredo's 2019 exit to Corpinnat. 100% Xarel·lo, 150+ months on lees, brut nature — the prestige Spanish counterweight to vintage Champagne in any traditional-method flight, and a curveball variety identification target (Xarel·lo can read as a long-lees Chardonnay/Champagne look-alike on the nose). Pairs with Pol Roger NV / Bollinger La Grande Année / Nyetimber Classic Cuvée for a four-country traditional-method spread.

tasting_profile.appearance: Surprisingly pale for its extreme age on lees (150+ months). Very fine, delicate bead with persistent mousse. Bright golden hue with green-gold reflexes in younger releases. Clarity is brilliant despite zero dosage and hand disgorgement without freezing.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Sea spray and chalky minerality lead, with hints of caramelized sugar from brief barrel fermentation. Dried leaves, balsamic bay leaf, and underbrush give a forest-floor complexity. Very fine pastry and marzipan notes from extended lees contact. Fennel, grapefruit, and lime citrus. Some vintages show sherry-like flor reminiscences — spicy curry and fennel notes (especially 2009). Toast, brioche, and subtle smoke in the distance. White flowers and herbaceous notes emerge with air. The wine is described as 'Burgundian and smoky' with great complexity.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Electric lime citrus fruit juxtaposed against a creamy weight of marzipan from 12+ years on lees. Toast, spice, and smoke remain as supporting characters. Lively and powerful despite the extreme age. The 2004 vintage retains special liveliness — complex and indefinable — with pronounced acidity, intensity, and persistence. Chalky, mineral, and winey character. Rich and sleek texture with coriander notes persisting. Honey on the palate with a lean, structured precision. The wine constantly evolves in the glass.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: High acidity providing remarkable freshness despite 150+ months on lees. Medium-plus body. Very fine bead. Brut Nature — zero dosage, zero added sugar. 11.5-12% ABV. Calcareous soil minerality evident throughout. Long and creamy finish. Balance of concentration and vivacity is the hallmark. Described by Robert Parker's Wine Advocate as 'subtle, elegant, complex and very well integrated' (96+ points, 2004 vintage).
sources: Robert Parker's Wine Advocate — 96+ points (2004 vintage), Luis Gutiérrez
Decanter — Wine Legend feature on 1999 vintage by Stephen Brook
Recaredo official website (recaredo.com) — tasting notes and vintage descriptions
Atlas Fine Wines — 2009 vintage review with detailed tasting notes

vintage_guidance: Multi-vintage flagship since 2016; current release blends multiple recent base years with ~25–35% reserve wines. Average ~30–36 months on lees before disgorgement.
appellation: PDO Sussex / English Quality Sparkling Wine
selection_notes: Reference English sparkling wine: same three Champagne varieties on Sussex chalk/greensand, 30+ months on lees, 12% ABV, brut dosage. The textbook "is it Champagne or English?" curveball — typically distinguishable by slightly higher acid, leaner fruit, and earlier release than vintage Champagne. Anchors the cross-country traditional-method flight alongside Pol Roger, Bollinger LGA, and Recaredo Turó d'en Mota.

tasting_profile.appearance: Pale to restrained golden hue — some reviewers note an amber tinge on older disgorgements. Fine, delicate bubbles with a gentle, elegant mousse. Bright clarity. Lighter in colour than vintage Champagne, reflecting the cooler climate and stainless-steel vinification.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Prominent toast, buttery brioche, and burnt toast from extended lees ageing (30-36 months). Nutty depth with almond undertones. Fresh-baked white bread. Complex citrus — lemon, green apple — with a hint of pineapple. Caramel and mature baked fruit. An earthy, mushroomy note provides additional complexity (Jancis Robinson). Spicy aromatics. The nose bridges the gap between autolytic richness and cool-climate freshness.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Layers of creamy, toasted flavours reminiscent of biscotti dipped in vanilla cream. Honey, almond, pastry, and baked apple provide the core. Fresh apple and citrus (green apple, lemon) add cool-climate energy. Brioche and nutty richness are indulgent without being overpowering. Light- to medium-bodied with crisp green-apple acidity. The finish features caramel and toast flavours with notable length. Intense yet delicate, elegant yet complex.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: High acidity (8.4-8.5 g/L) — notably higher than most Champagne, giving the wine its characteristic English sparkling tension and freshness. Light to medium body. Fine, soft bubbles — never harsh or overly fizzy. 12% ABV. Residual sugar 8.5-10 g/L (Brut). pH 3.0-3.27. Very long finish. The higher acidity and leaner fruit profile are the key distinguishing markers from Champagne in blind tasting.
sources: Nyetimber official website (nyetimber.com) — producer tasting notes and technical data
Jancis Robinson — 'delicious bakery and fresh-apple aromas, plus a wonderful earthy, mushroomy note'
English Wine Guide (englishwine.blog) — detailed review December 2024
Robert Parker's Wine Advocate — 90 points, noting 'crisp green-apple energy'
Glass of Bubbly — review noting citrus and pineapple on the nose

vintage_guidance: Non-vintage solera. Current bottlings draw from criaderas y solera maintained since 1844; minimum 4 years biological aging under flor.
sub_region: Jerez de la Frontera (Macharnudo and Carrascal vineyards, Jerez Superior pago)
classification: Fino — biologically aged Sherry, criaderas y solera system
selection_notes: Globally iconic Fino — the calibration reference for biologically aged Sherry. Bone-dry, 15% fortification, ≥4 years under flor in albariza-grown Palomino. Use as the dry/biological end of a Jerez ladder (Fino → Amontillado → Oloroso → PX) or as a fortified-white curveball where candidates must distinguish flor character (saline, yeasty, almond, bitter) from oxidative aging (Lustau Don Nuño foil).

tasting_profile.appearance: Pale golden yellow — notably light due to the protective blanket of flor preventing oxidation during 4+ years of biological ageing. Brilliant clarity. Legs are light, consistent with the light body and 15% ABV. The colour is a key diagnostic in blind tasting — far paler than Amontillado or Oloroso from the same region.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Fresh and bright, with prominent notes of lemons, green olives, and a gentle saline touch. Yeasty, bready aromas from flor biological ageing — the hallmark Fino character. Almonds (typical of Palomino), dried flowers, and the softest medicinal/iodine touch. Apple fruit. Sharp yet elegant flor character. The en rama versions add notes of albero sand, esparto grass, and more pronounced nutty undertones. Pungent flor aroma is immediately identifiable.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Immediately dry — bone-dry with zero sweetness. Savoury and salty, like salted almonds and green olives. Creamy texture despite the dryness. Crisp and fresh when served cold. Marzipan, yeasty brie-cheese-rind notes. Grapefruit acidity and juicy lemon. Medium-dry to medium-bodied on the palate with moderate salinity building toward the finish. Clear acetaldehyde notes. The finish is salty and slightly bitter — a diagnostic Fino marker.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: High acidity providing spine and zest. Light body. 15% ABV (fortified). Bone-dry. Pronounced salinity on the mid-palate and finish. Slight bitter edge on the finish. Long finish driven by citrus acidity and salt rather than fruit. The combination of high acid, salinity, bitter finish, and pungent flor yeast character is the textbook Fino structural fingerprint — unmistakable in blind tasting.
sources: SherryNotes (sherrynotes.com) — Ruben's review of Tío Pepe and Tío Pepe En Rama 2022
González Byass / Tío Pepe official (tiopepe.com) — producer tasting notes
Wine Enthusiast — 89 points, noting 'popping acidity' and 'salty finish'
Wine Advocate (Luis Gutiérrez) — 90-91+ points on en rama versions
88 Bamboo — detailed tasting notes: 'savoury and salty, like salted almonds and green olives'

vintage_guidance: Non-vintage solera. Average age in solera: ~12 years of fully oxidative aging. Bodega La Campana, Jerez de la Frontera.
sub_region: Jerez de la Frontera (Bodega La Campana)
selection_notes: Mid-tier dry Oloroso — fortified at 20% to suppress flor, ~12 years of pure oxidative aging in American oak. Palette anchor for the oxidative end of the Jerez ladder against Tío Pepe (biological flor) — the same Palomino base under two opposed aging regimes makes this the textbook MW exam Sherry-style discrimination pair. Useful in any fortified flight or as a dry / nutty / rancio curveball against PX, Cream Sherry, or aged Tawny Port.

tasting_profile.appearance: Dark bronze colour with a golden rim. Medium-intensity brown with amber highlights and gold glints. Clear and bright. Notably deeper than Fino or Amontillado — the fully oxidative ageing without flor produces the characteristic dark, concentrated colour that is a primary diagnostic for Oloroso in blind tasting.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Pungent nutty aromas — roasted walnut, toasted almond, hazelnut, and marzipan dominate. Smoky wood background. Dried fig, prune, dark caramel, and leather lie underneath. Rich complex of charred wood, maple syrup, mushroom, cardamom, cocoa, and Assam tea. Tobacco leaf, cedar, and warm spice (nutmeg, clove). Booze-soaked plump sultana. Pronounced rancio character. A lemon zing cuts through the oxidative complexity. The nose is intensely aromatic — medium-plus to high intensity.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Full-bodied and concentrated. Rich layers of dried fruit — fig, raisin, prune — drenched in walnut oil, dark chocolate, molasses, and espresso roast. Bitter chocolate, walnuts, and baked chestnuts are the core flavours. Oxidative character brings spicy wood soot and stewed citrus peel. Sultana and some raisin with a cleansing grip. Dry despite the rich, generous texture. Mouthcoating texture. Robert Parker describes 'nuttiness (walnuts) intermixed with a hint of tea and salty, caramelized flavors.'
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Medium-plus acidity — providing a tangy, cleansing lift that lightens the concentrated aftertaste. Full body. 20% ABV (fortified higher than Fino to suppress flor). Dry (under 8 g/L RS). Medium-plus flavour intensity. Long, dry, persistent finish with lingering nutty tobacco leaf and spice. The combination of full body, dryness, high alcohol, walnut-rancio character, and tangy acidity is the textbook dry Oloroso structural profile.
sources: Difford's Guide — tasting review sampled April 2025: 'bronze amber with gold glints, pungent walnuts and tobacco'
Robert Parker — 94 points: 'walnuts intermixed with tea, salty caramelized flavors'
Lustau official via Vintage Imports (vntgimports.com) — producer tasting notes
Wine Casual — 94 points: 'roasted walnut, prune, caramel, fig, toffee, butterscotch, nutmeg, toast, marmalade'
Natalie MacLean (Greg Hughes review) — 90 points: 'beautiful amber, charred wood, maple syrup, mushroom, cardamom, cocoa, Assam tea'

vintage_guidance: Non-vintage; blend of cask-aged tawnies with an average of 20 years in seasoned 630-litre oak casks at Taylor's Vila Nova de Gaia lodges. Continuously bottled to date of release.
sub_region: Cima Corgo and Douro Superior (vineyards) / Vila Nova de Gaia (lodges)
classification: Aged Tawny Port — 20 Years Old (Categoria de 20 Anos)
selection_notes: Iconic 20-Year-Old Tawny — Taylor's reference style, the textbook aged-Tawny benchmark. Sweet (~111 g/L), 20% ABV, fully oxidative cask-aged blend showing dried fig, walnut, orange peel, and rancio. Producer chosen deliberately to diversify away from Quinta do Noval (cited in 2022 P3 Q3). Use as the oxidative-aged Port pole against a hypothetical reductive Vintage Port, or against Madeira (Bual) and aged dry Oloroso in a fortified flight where the candidate must distinguish sweet vs dry, oxidative vs biological, mutage vs flor.

tasting_profile.appearance: Intense amber tawny colour — a characteristic hue between deep ruby and full amber, with bright cherry reflections at the rim. Clear and bright. The colour is diagnostic for aged Tawny — distinctly different from the deep purple-black of young Ruby/Vintage Port, having lost its primary red colour through extended oxidative cask ageing over ~20 years.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Opulent and voluptuous nose of complex spicy, jammy, and nutty aromas. Toasted walnut, black espresso, almond, and hazelnut form the nutty core. Hints of orange flower and orange peel. Fine oakiness from the long period in 630-litre seasoned casks. Buttery caramel, dates, and fruitcake. Sweet decomposing autumn leaves with wood notes. Toffee, vanilla bean, and dried citrus rind. Prune and butterscotch. The nose is intensely aromatic and immediately signals aged Tawny Port.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Full-bodied with very rich and concentrated flavour. Luxardo maraschino cherry and walnut blend on the palate. Molasses, espresso, Nutella, raisin, and date. Burnt caramel, rich buttery date squares, dried Montmorency cherry, dried apricot, and marmalade. Slightly bitter hazelnut on the finish. The mouthfeel is nicely balanced, smooth, viscous, and rich. Sweet yet with medium-plus acidity preventing cloying sweetness. The flavours linger extensively.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Medium-plus acidity (5.3 g/L) providing freshness against the sweetness. Full body. 20% ABV (fortified with grape spirit/aguardente). Sweet — approximately 111 g/L residual sugar. Subtle, integrated tannins — no need for decanting. Long, mellow finish. Smooth, velvety texture. The balance of sweetness, acidity, nuttiness, and oxidative complexity at 20% ABV is the hallmark aged Tawny profile. Serve slightly chilled (12-16°C).
sources: Taylor Fladgate official (taylor.pt) — producer tasting notes and technical data
WineQuest technical sheet — ABV 20%, acidity 5.3 g/L, RS 111.17 g/L
Ken's Wine Guide — 96 points (October 2025): 'toasted walnut, black espresso, Luxardo cherry'
Natalie MacLean (multiple sommeliers) — 90-94 points: 'amber with cherry reflections, buttery caramel, dates, fruitcake'
James Suckling — 95 points: 'dried berries, light chocolate, nuts, honey, toffee, caramel'

vintage_guidance: Non-vintage blend with average age of 10 years. Range was reformulated and rebottled in 2024 by winemaker Francisco Albuquerque using the Canteiro system across two ageing locations (Funchal Wine Lodge and Caniçal winery).
sub_region: South coast of the island (Ribeira Brava to Calheta)
classification: Aged Madeira — 10 Years Old, single grape variety (Bual / Boal), medium-rich
selection_notes: Reference medium-rich Madeira — 100% Bual, 10 years Canteiro ageing, 19% ABV, ~95 g/L RS. Producer chosen to diversify away from Henriques & Henriques (heavily featured in 2021 P3 Q3). Sits in the middle of the Madeira sweetness ladder (Sercial → Verdelho → Bual → Malmsey) and provides the textbook "oxidative-but-not-rancio" benchmark — Madeira shares solera-like multi-decade ageing logic with Sherry/Port but adds the heat-and-time signature of the Atlantic island cellar. Pairs with Taylor 20yr Tawny and Lustau Don Nuño in any fortified-flight identification question.

tasting_profile.appearance: Clear amber colour with tinges of gold on the rim. Medium-deep intensity. The Canteiro-aged colour is characteristic of medium-rich Madeira — darker than Sercial or Verdelho but lighter than Malmsey. Brilliant clarity after fining.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Superbly complex with great intensity. Bouquet of dried fruits — figs, prunes, and raisins dominate. Notes of almonds, oak, and subtle hints of toffee and vanilla spice. Butterscotch. Seville orange marmalade, quince, beeswax, dried orange peel, and cardamom (Wine Advocate). Floral notes. Walnuts and orange confit. Rancio and tea notes develop with age. The nose is immediately identifiable as Madeira through its combination of dried fruit, citrus peel, and the distinctive heat-aged complexity.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Medium sweet and very smooth. Mouth-filling concentrated flavours of maple syrup, figs, caramel, and sugared walnuts. Superb balance of coffee and fruit flavours. Sumptuous texture. Subtle marmalade and spice notes on the entry. Sweet but with a characteristic streak of acid verve that is the Madeira hallmark — like honey with lemon juice. The palate is round, ample, and pleasing. Clean, sharp acidity cuts through the sweetness. Lovely persistent aftertaste that finishes more fresh and dry than sweet.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: High acidity (7 g/L total acidity) — significantly higher than Port at comparable sweetness, and the key Madeira identifier in blind tasting. Medium-plus body. 19% ABV (fortified to arrest fermentation). Medium sweet — approximately 95-100 g/L residual sugar. Baumé 3.4. Canteiro ageing (natural attic heat, no estufagem). Endless finish with lovely complexity. The acid-sweetness tension is the diagnostic feature: sweeter than Verdelho but with more acid nerve than Malmsey.
sources: IWSC — Silver Medal: 'Packed with aromas of raisins, butterscotch and vanilla. Maple syrup, figs, caramel and sugared walnuts.'
Wine Advocate (Robert Parker) — 89 points, 2016: 'Seville orange marmalade, quince, beeswax, dried orange peel, cardamom'
Wine Spectator — 91 points: 'amber with gold rim, dried fruits, figs, prunes, almonds, toffee, vanilla spice'
IWC — Silver Medal: 'Floral nose. Walnuts, fig and orange confit. Apricot and marmalade, rancio and tea notes.'
Blandy's official technical sheet (bottlebooks/blandys.com) — technical specifications and IWSC tasting note

vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2013, 2017 and 2018 widely circulating. Producer publishes per-vintage technical data: e.g. 2013 declared at 11% abv, 155.7 g/L residual sugar, 7.9 g/L total acidity; 2017 cited as a textbook botrytis year with three weeks of morning mists in October; 2018 record-breaking warm dry vintage with sporadic showers driving noble rot.
sub_region: Mád / Mezőzombor (estate vineyards on volcanic and clay-loess soils)
classification: Tokaji Aszú 5 Puttonyos (legal minimum 120 g/L residual sugar under post-2013 PDO rules; on the label this is the second-highest Puttonyos classification below 6 Puttonyos and Aszú Eszencia)
rs_level: ~150-160 g/L typical (155.7 g/L declared on 2013 vintage; minimum 120 g/L for 5 Puttonyos)
selection_notes: Definitive Tokaji Aszú 5 Puttonyos anchor for any P3 sweet flight. Royal Tokaji is the founding modern post-1990 producer (Hugh Johnson + investors). Use to differentiate Tokaji from Sauternes — note the Furmint + Hárslevelű blend, far higher acidity (7-9 g/L vs Sauternes ~4-5 g/L), botrytis berries macerated INTO fermenting base wine rather than vinified together, ageing in 300/500 L Hungarian oak fudders not Bordeaux 225 L barriques. Perfect foil to Coutet Barsac in a botrytis cross-country flight or alongside an Egon Müller BA in a sweetness-vs-mechanism trio.

tasting_profile.appearance: Light to medium golden colour, clear and bright. Viscous texture visible in the glass — legs are slow and pronounced, reflecting the very high residual sugar (150-160 g/L). Deeper gold in older vintages. The colour is lighter than Sauternes at comparable sweetness levels, a useful blind-tasting discriminator.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Intense nose with lots of fruitiness and spice. Apricot jam and dried apricot are the signature Tokaji markers. Peach, white peach, and ripe plums. Honey and tangerine. Mango and passion fruit in riper vintages (2018). Ground cherries and slight dill (2016). Ginger, saffron, and marzipan from botrytis influence. Delicate floral aspect. Simple syrup. Fine oak and nutty frame from Hungarian oak cask maturation. Orange rind. The combination of apricot-honey-saffron is the classic Tokaji Aszú aromatic fingerprint.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Luscious and sensuous with layers of apricot jam, tangerine, honey, ginger, and marzipan. White peach and ripe plum flavours in the 2017 vintage. Orange rind with a slight bitter quality that works well as a counterpoint. Full-bodied and viscous. Rich yet silky on the tongue — not heavy or cloying. Good acidity counters the sweetness, creating the characteristic Tokaji tension. Slight minty note in some vintages. Long finish with a slight bitter orange-rind quality. The wine should drink well for 20+ years.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Very high acidity (7.9 g/L in 2013; 7.93 g/L in 2017) — dramatically higher than Sauternes (typically 4-5 g/L) and the single most important Tokaji identifier in blind tasting. Medium alcohol at 11% ABV (not fortified — fermentation stopped naturally by sugar concentration). Very high residual sugar — 155-160 g/L typical (minimum 120 g/L for 5 Puttonyos classification). Full body. Long, lingering finish. The acidity-sweetness balance is extraordinary — the wine tastes fresher and less cloying than its RS suggests. Botrytis character adds ginger/saffron complexity absent in non-botrytized sweet wines.
sources: Royal Tokaji official (royal-tokaji.com) — 2017 vintage: 'lots of fruitiness and spice, vibrant freshness, white peach, ripe plums'
Natalie MacLean (Jane Staples, Certified Sommelier) — 95 points, 2017: 'apricot jam, tangerine, honey, ginger, marzipan'
IntoWine — 2016 review: 'peaches, apricots, simple syrup, orange rind, good acidity, long finish'
CellarTracker — 2018: 'ripe stone fruit, tropical fruit, mango/passion fruit, fine oak and nutty frame'
Food Perestroika — 2005/2006 review: 'apricot and honey are the two most recurring Tokaji notes'

vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; recent classic vintages include 2015, 2016, 2017 and 2019. Owned and managed by the Baly family; Baron Philippe de Rothschild S.A. holds exclusive worldwide distribution.
sub_region: Sauternes-Barsac (commune of Barsac, between the Garonne and Ciron rivers)
classification: Premier Cru Classé (1855 Bordeaux Classification, Sauternes-Barsac section — alongside Climens, Suduiraut, Rieussec, Coutet etc.; below the lone Premier Cru Supérieur d'Yquem)
rs_level: sweet (Barsac Premier Cru style typically 110-150 g/L residual sugar)
selection_notes: Mid-tier 1er Cru Classé Sauternes anchor — deliberately NOT d'Yquem or Guiraud (the two most-cited names) to add diversity. Coutet sits in Barsac sub-appellation (clay-limestone, knife-edge acidity hinted in the name 'couteau'), 75% Sémillon/23% Sauv Blanc/2% Muscadelle, 38-44 ha, 18 months in 70-100% new French oak, average 9 hL/ha yields, manual harvest in multiple tries. Use as: (i) the Bordeaux/Sauternes reference against a Tokaji Aszú or Mosel BA in a botrytis trio; (ii) part of a Sémillon-led spread (vs Hunter Valley Tyrrell's Vat 1); (iii) Barsac-specific question (linking with 1855 classification).

tasting_profile.appearance: Deep golden to old-gold colour with brilliant clarity; pronounced legs indicating high viscosity and residual sugar concentration. With age, evolves toward deep amber-gold with bright, luminous reflections.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Immediately expressive with honey-drenched pineapple, candied orange rind, apricot, and mango. Layered floral notes of honeysuckle and orange blossom. Vanilla and saffron from 70-100% new French oak. Older vintages develop toffee, caramel, and dried-fruit complexity. A distinctive peppery, spicy note from Muscadelle is evident in some vintages.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Lush, generous, and creamy on entry with streaming flavours of pineapple, yellow apple, green plum, and white ginger. Candied citrus, preserved apricot, and tropical fruit dominate the mid-palate. The Barsac limestone signature delivers a knife-edge freshness (the estate name derives from 'couteau' meaning knife) that cuts through the richness, preventing cloying sweetness. Long, persistent finish with citrus zest and honeyed mineral notes.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Medium-plus acidity (Barsac's limestone soils deliver higher acid than most Sauternes); full body; very high residual sugar (110-150 g/L typical); 13.5% abv; pronounced viscosity; exceptionally long finish. 2014 noted as having highest combined acid + alcohol in 20+ years. Ageing potential 20-40+ years for top vintages.

vintage_guidance: Produced only in exceptional botrytis vintages and in tiny quantities. The 2024 release is the first Scharzhofberger Beerenauslese since 2015 — these wines are extreme rarities. Recent BA-producing years include 2003, 2005, 2007, 2010, 2015, 2024. Distributed exclusively through select merchants and the VDP Grosser Ring auction in Trier; estate does not sell direct.
sub_region: Wiltingen / Saar — Scharzhofberg vineyard (devonian blue slate, south-facing, considered Grand Cru equivalent of the Saar)
classification: VDP.Grosse Lage — Prädikat: Beerenauslese (legal Oechsle minimum ~110-128° depending on region; botrytis berry-by-berry selection)
rs_level: sweet (BA band typically 150-220 g/L for Mosel BA; Egon Müller 2024 noted as lower-band BA-style but still classified as BA after acid-stopped fermentation)
selection_notes: The apex of the Mosel Prädikat ladder — a true MW-exam curveball when paired with Tokaji and Sauternes in a botrytis trio. Note the contrast: Mosel BA is 100% Riesling, fermentation arrests naturally at 7-8% abv (vs ~13.5% Sauternes / ~11% Tokaji), residual sugar similar or higher, but acidity is the highest of the three (~9-11 g/L) — this acid-sugar tension is the Mosel signature. Deliberately distinct from the Dr Loosen and JJ Prüm entries already in the bank (Kabinett/Spätlese tier). Egon Müller produces these only in exceptional years; the 2024 is the first BA since 2015. Source confidence is medium because the estate does not publish a public BA tech sheet — sourcing relies on detailed Lobenberg retailer notes plus producer biography pages and JancisRobinson coverage.

tasting_profile.appearance: Burnished gold to deep old-gold colour; brilliant clarity with pronounced, viscous legs indicating extreme residual sugar concentration and low alcohol. With significant age (decades), evolves toward orange-brown hues while retaining luminosity.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Intensely perfumed and utterly intoxicating bouquet: acacia honey, chamomile, marmalade, Japanese yuzu, candied apricot, and saffron. Characteristic Saar slate minerality underpins. With age, develops lychee, anise, green herbs, petrol/diesel notes (classic aged Riesling), and warm caramel. Delicate floral element persists throughout evolution. The bouquet is extraordinarily harmonious and never overpowering.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Unctuous on entry with effortless concentration. Creme brulee, vanilla, quince, chamomile, orange pith, and bitter orange. The hallmark killer line of acidity offsets extreme sweetness, creating the defining Saar acid-sugar tension. While still young, can taste very sweet, but over decades develops unrivalled complexity. The wine never demands attention on the finish -- far too sophisticated for ostentation. Ridiculous length.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Very high acidity (9-11 g/L, highest of any botrytis type); extreme residual sugar (150-220 g/L BA band); very low alcohol (7-8%); light to medium body despite sugar concentration; wet-slate minerality; finish of extraordinary persistence measured in minutes. Ageing potential: effectively limitless -- 50+ years demonstrated (1971 vintage rated 97/100 after 47 years by Vinous).

vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2024 and 2025 currently in market. 2024 noted as exceptionally dry — the driest Marlborough season in 30+ years — slowing ripening and helping retain balance. Founded 1985 by David Hohnen of Cape Mentelle, now owned by LVMH.
sub_region: Wairau Valley (Rapaura, Renwick, Brancott Valley sub-regions) — key blocks Estate, Motukawa, Widow's Block, Red Shed, Mustang
selection_notes: The single most globally recognized Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc — founded 1985 by David Hohnen, now LVMH-owned, set the world standard for the Marlborough thiolic style. Mandatory anchor for any cross-country Sauv Blanc spread (vs Sancerre, vs Chilean Errazuriz already in bank, vs Pessac-Léognan, vs Marlborough Bordeaux blend). Wairau Valley gravelly free-draining soils, exceptional sunshine hours, marked diurnals → passionfruit/gooseberry/lime/white nectarine signature with sea-salt edge. Use to contrast with a flinty/reductive Sancerre or a textural Greywacke alternative; or as a commercial benchmark foil at the value end of an NZ flight.

tasting_profile.appearance: Almost clear to pale lemon-green with water-white rim; brilliant clarity; light, fine legs indicating moderate alcohol and no residual sugar. Youthful, bright, and vibrant in the glass.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Immediately expressive and enticing with passionfruit, ripe gooseberry, lime curd, kaffir/makrut lime leaf, and white nectarine. Fresh-cut grass and tomato leaf add a herbaceous dimension. Pink peppercorn, hibiscus, and orange blossom provide aromatic complexity. Citrus notes of yellow grapefruit, lemon zest, and pineapple. Crushed basil and green olive in some vintages. 2024 (40th Vintage) noted as more restrained and less tropical than previous vintages, with profound citrus, apricot, and orange blossom.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Vibrant, mouthwatering intensity with concentrated, juicy tropical notes (passionfruit, pineapple, mango) melding with zesty citrus and stone fruit. Lemon curd and white peach mid-palate. Underpinned by lively grapefruit acidity carrying intense fruit to an impressively long finish. Fresh sea-salt saline edge and squeezed-lemon acidity. Red capsicum and green olive add savoury complexity. Hints of beeswax and wet stone provide textural interest.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: High acidity (crisp, mouthwatering); medium body; dry; 13.5% abv; moderate viscosity; impressively long finish for the style. Fresh and vibrant with no oak influence on the flagship cuvee (96%+ stainless steel, with only ~4% old French oak/concrete for texture). Drink young, within 1-3 years of vintage.
sources: https://buywinesonline.com/products/cloudy-bay-sauvignon-blanc-2025-750-ml (Wine Spectator and James Suckling notes on 2025)

vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2023 and 2024 widely available. Producer publishes per-vintage tech sheets with full analytical detail. 2024 reviewed by JancisRobinson team as 'slightly slimmer and more lithe' vs 2023. First vintage of this wine: 1994.
sub_region: Blend across Lunlunta (Maipú, ~920 m), Gualtallary (Tupungato, ~1,450 m), and El Cepillo/La Consulta/Eugenio Bustos (Uco Valley, ~1,090 m)
classification: Estate-tier cuvée (Catena's mainstream multi-vineyard blend; one tier above the regional Alamos line and below Catena Alta, Catena Zapata Malbec Argentino, and Adrianna Vineyard parcel wines)
selection_notes: The mainstream/value Malbec anchor — deliberately the entry-level Catena Estate cuvée (~$15-25 retail) to create a price-and-quality ladder against the Catena Adrianna Vineyard River Malbec already in the bank (~$190-250). Same producer, same variety, same broad region, but the Estate blend smooths multiple altitudes (Lunlunta lowland + Gualtallary high + La Consulta mid) into a softer commercial expression vs Adrianna's single high-altitude parcel intensity. Excellent for: (i) Argentina price ladder questions; (ii) cross-country Malbec spreads (Catena vs Cahors Cosse-Maisonneuve / Lagrézette); (iii) Mendoza commercial anchor in any P2 reds flight. ID deliberately distinct from the existing Adrianna entry.

tasting_profile.appearance: Deep garnet to dark purple-ruby core with a narrow ruby-violet rim; mostly opaque; bright and clean. Pronounced legs indicating ripe fruit and moderate-plus alcohol.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Incredibly aromatic with prominent rose and violet florals. Ripe plum, blackberry, black cherry, and raspberry jam. Soft earthiness of browned leaves and tobacco. Red licorice and balsamic notes. Light vanilla and soft oak from French barrel ageing. A characteristic touch of initial funk on opening that blossoms quickly to reveal vibrant, saturated fruit. Some vintages show spice and herbs (cumin, pepper) alongside the dark fruit.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Rich and mouth-filling with plum, blackberry, black cherry, and raspberry. Violet florals carry through from the nose. Balsamic and soft chocolate notes from oak integration. Leathery dark fruit with earthy tobacco undertones. Juicy and fresh with vibrant fruit saturation. Medium to full body with a plush, smooth texture. The altitude freshness from high Andes vineyards (920-1450m) provides a distinctive lift and bracing vigour. Finish is dry, slightly tannic, and impressively long-lasting.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Medium-plus acidity (altitude-driven freshness); medium-plus tannins (fine-grained, soft, well-integrated); medium-plus to full body; 13-13.5% abv; dry; long finish. Cold maceration 5 days at 10C, 10-16 day ferment, 19-22 day total maceration, 11-13 months French oak (mix of new and used). Robert Parker Wine Advocate 92 points (2021 vintage). Drink now to 5-7 years.
sources: https://88bamboo.co/blogs/wine-reviews/catena-malbec-catena-zapata (detailed 2021 tasting with colour, aroma, taste, finish)

vintage_guidance: Non-vintage / current-release blended NV style. World's #1 Vinho Verde by volume per producer; distributed in 70+ countries. Tech sheet publishes 80 hL/ha average yield, 6.5 g/L tartaric total acidity, pH 2.8-3.4, 11 g/L residual sugar.
sub_region: Vinho Verde DOC (sourced across the region via grower partnerships into the Aveleda vinification centre)
rs_level: 11 g/L (just off-dry in profile, technically dry by EU labelling)
selection_notes: The default value white anchor — distinct from the Soalheiro Alvarinho premium entry already in the bank (which is single-variety, premium Monção). Casal Garcia is the world's #1-selling Vinho Verde, a multi-grape blend (Trajadura, Loureiro, Pedernã, Azal) at 9.5% abv, 11 g/L RS, slight petillance, ~$8 retail. Use as: (i) the commercial value-price floor in any P1 white flight; (ii) the cheap NV-style anchor in a Portugal flight vs Soalheiro / Niepoort; (iii) a low-alcohol curveball where the slight spritz and 9.5% abv directs candidates away from neutral varieties like Pinot Grigio; (iv) the unmistakable signal of granite-soils, multi-grape Vinho Verde — a classic IMW broad-commercial-anchor question.

tasting_profile.appearance: Pale lime-juice to lemon-yellow colour with greenish tints; clear and limpid appearance; very fine, slight petillance visible as tiny bubbles clinging to glass. Light, watery legs indicating low alcohol and body.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Delicate and fruity with notes of citrus (lemon, lime), green apple, and light white flowers. A subtle, youthful, fresh aroma profile that is clean and inviting without complexity. Wine Enthusiast notes it as 'all bright, clean fruit.' The nose is approachable and light, reflecting its role as an aperitif-style wine.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Fresh, smooth, and well-balanced with a crisp, sprite-like quality from the slight CO2 petillance. Lemon-lime and green apple dominate, with a saline, mineral edge. Just off-dry (11 g/L RS) but refreshing rather than sweet, balanced by bracing acidity. Clean, simple, and cheerful with a short to medium-length finish. The slight fizz amplifies the refreshing character and makes it particularly agreeable chilled.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: High acidity (6.5 g/L tartaric, pH 2.8-3.4); light body; low alcohol (9.5%); slight petillance (added CO2); just off-dry (11 g/L RS, technically dry by EU labelling); short to medium finish. 85 points Wine Enthusiast. Drink immediately on release; not for ageing.

vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2020, 2022, 2023 widely circulating. Producer publishes a tech sheet; 2020 importer (Vintus) PDF cites traditional long maceration in stainless steel with twice-daily pump-overs, and 24-30 months in neutral oak (this differs slightly from producer page which states 18 months in foudres — both descriptions are sourced; difference is between 'collection' bottlings vs vintage variation). ~4.5 million bottles produced annually.
sub_region: Côtes du Rhône (broad regional sourcing across the southern Rhône; Guigal house in Ampuis, northern Rhône)
selection_notes: The reference value red anchor — Guigal Côtes du Rhône Rouge is the most-shipped Rhône red in the world (~4.5 million bottles/year, retails ~$13-18). Deliberately Syrah-led (50% Syrah / 40% Grenache / 10% Mourvèdre) — atypical for a Southern Rhône blend which is more commonly Grenache-dominant — and aged 18-30 months in neutral oak / foudres, far longer than competitors at the price. This is a classic MW exam curveball: the structure says serious producer, the price band says value, the blend ratio whispers 'northern Rhône influence on a southern Rhône AOC.' Use as: (i) the value-end anchor in a P2 reds flight; (ii) Rhône AOC ladder question (CdR → CdR Villages → Gigondas → Châteauneuf); (iii) cross-country GSM/Syrah-blend comparison (vs South African Stellenbosch, vs Aussie Barossa GSM, vs Spanish Garnacha-led).

tasting_profile.appearance: Deep and dark red to purple-ruby with a narrow ruby rim; mostly opaque and bright; shiny. Good viscosity indicating ripe fruit concentration and moderate-plus alcohol.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Intensely aromatic with expressive blackberry, blueberry, and black cherry fruit. Bouquet garni and garrigue herbs. Peppery spice (black pepper especially), violet florals, and a touch of campfire smoke. Notes of lilac, dark cherries, and flowers. Slight earthiness and black olive undertones. Dusty, savoury quality alongside the fruit. The elevated Syrah content (50%) drives aromatic intensity beyond typical Grenache-led Cotes du Rhone.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Full, round, and 'sexy' per the producer. Smooth and supple with round, velvety tannins. Blackberry and red berry fruit with peppery spice, garrigue, and black olive. Earth and tobacco notes ground the fruit. Medium to full-bodied with remarkable harmony and textured mouthfeel. Savoury, slightly drying finish with a burst of spice at the very end and impressive length. Wine Advocate notes it as 'supple and fluid, easy to drink, with black olive and earth notes preventing simple fruit bomb.'
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Medium-plus acidity; medium-plus tannins (round, smooth, well-balanced); medium-plus to full body; 14-15% abv (vintage dependent); dry; long finish with elegance. 50% Syrah / 40% Grenache / 10% Mourvedre; aged 24-30 months in neutral oak foudres -- far longer than peers at this price. 90-92 points across Jeb Dunnuck, Wine Advocate, Wine Enthusiast, Decanter.
sources: https://vintus.com/wines/cotes-du-rhone-rouge/e-guigal-cotes-du-rhone-rouge-2022 (Vintus 2022 tech sheet + Wine Advocate 90, Jeb Dunnuck 91, Wine Enthusiast 91)
https://vintus.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/Guigal-CDR-Rouge-20-Tech.pdf (2020 tech sheet PDF with Wine Advocate 90)

vintage_guidance: Universally declared classic vintage — 2017 was only the second time in 144 years that consecutive years (2016 and 2017) were both universally declared. Field blend aged ~20 months in oak vats at Vila Nova de Gaia before bottling, then bottle-aged reductively for decades. Drinking window 2030+ per producer.
sub_region: Cima Corgo and Douro Superior (Quinta de Vargellas, Quinta de Terra Feita, Quinta do Junco); aged at Vila Nova de Gaia lodges
selection_notes: Iconic declared-vintage classic Port — the reductive bottle-aged pole to set against the already-banked Taylor Fladgate 20yr Tawny (same producer, opposite ageing regime). 2017 was a universally declared vintage (only 5 in the 21st century pre-2017), giving the candidate a textbook 'declared year' anchor. Field blend of Vargellas, Terra Feita, Junco; ~20 months in vat, ~100 g/L RS, 20% ABV. In a fortified flight, this is the high-tannin, deep-purple, structured-youthful Port that must be distinguished from aged Tawny (mahogany, rancio, dried fig) and from Madeira (Atlantic baked spice). Quinta do Noval deliberately avoided per 2022 P3 Q3 corpus appearance.

tasting_profile.appearance: Dark inky purple-black with a narrow brick rim and cerise highlights at the edge; nearly opaque; very dense. Pronounced viscous legs indicating high sugar, alcohol, and extract concentration.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Subdued and broody in youth -- tight and restrained. Dark inky fruit with flashes of cassis, concentrated blackcurrant, black cherry, and plum. Bold black stone fruit. Violet florals, licorice, cedar, and tobacco. Subtle menthol/eucalyptus notes. Youthful spirit evident but integrated. Described as 'quintessential Taylor -- very austere and very precise' by winemaker David Guimaraens. Opens with time to reveal greater complexity.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Powerful and structured with massive, chewy tannins that are softly textured despite their volume. Noticeably sweet on entry until the dry tannins kick in. Multiple layers of dark fruit -- blackberry preserve, Pontefract cake (licorice), creme de cassis, black plums. Chocolate, menthol, and violet notes. The palate is full-bodied, off-dry in perception despite ~100 g/L RS, and very long. Searing acidity provides lift and freshness. A hint of black pepper on the grip. Dusty, drying tannins linger with the fruit on an impressively vigorous finish.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: High tannin (massive, firm, slightly austere, but fine-grained and chewy); medium-plus acidity (exceptional for the style); full body; ~100 g/L residual sugar; 20% abv; dense and concentrated; exceptionally long finish. From a universally declared vintage. Drinking window 2030+ per producer. 96-98+ points from specialist Port forums; described as 'a step up from the 2016.'

vintage_guidance: Single-vintage 'cosecha' Gran Reserva bottling. Pedro Ximénez grapes harvested mid-August 1999, sun-dried on esparto mats for 7–9 days to concentrate sugars, pressed and fortified, then aged for over 20 years in American oak butts before bottling. The Gran Reserva tier carries a minimum 25-year oxidative ageing benchmark for Toro Albalá's Don PX line.
sub_region: Aguilar de la Frontera (Córdoba); fruit from Sierra de Montilla and Moriles Altos superior pagos
classification: Vintage 'Cosecha' Gran Reserva — sweet Pedro Ximénez, oxidatively aged
selection_notes: Reference benchmark for sun-dried Pedro Ximénez — the extreme-sweet pole of the Andalusian fortified ladder. From Montilla-Moriles (where the bulk of PX historically came from; Toro Albalá was the first to bottle dessert PX themselves in 1970 rather than sell in bulk to Jerez bodegas), so it adds geographic and appellation diversity vs the existing Jerez entries (Tío Pepe Fino, Lustau Don Nuño Oloroso). At ~450 g/L RS, 17% ABV, and 20+ years in American oak butts, it provides the textbook 'molasses, fig, raisin, dark chocolate, coffee' PX profile any candidate must recognise on the P3 sweet axis. Producer chosen deliberately to diversify away from González Byass (already in bank as Tío Pepe) and Lustau (already in bank).

tasting_profile.appearance: Dark mahogany to almost black with beautiful viscous movement in the glass; intensely concentrated, syrupy consistency. Virtually opaque at the core with a thin amber-brown rim. When poured, coats the glass thickly like molasses.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Intensely sweet with raisined fruits -- dates, plums, dried peaches, and dried figs. Roasted coffee and coffee bean notes dominate. Dark chocolate and liquorice. Orange rind and citric sensation providing lift. Balsamic and cinnamon/vanilla sweet spice. Curiously perfumed for a PX -- Wine Advocate noted 'a floral touch that at times reminded me of Moscatel.' Molasses and caramel undertones throughout.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: Sweet entry that is perfectly balanced with citrus acidity and a light bitter touch, making it feel fresh despite extreme age and sweetness. Velvety and seamless with a soft, unctuous texture. Tasty, showy and easy to drink despite ~402 g/L sugar. Rich dried fruit, dark chocolate, coffee, and orange rind flavours. Wine Advocate noted it 'feels lighter and less concentrated' than expected, with an elegant drinkability. Long, persistent, lingering sweet finish with orange peel and bitter coffee notes providing complexity.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Low to medium acidity (balancing freshness from citric acid and bitter touch); full body; very sweet (~402 g/L residual sugar per Wine Advocate); very viscous; 17% abv; exceptionally long finish. Sun-dried Pedro Ximenez grapes, 7-9 days on esparto mats, 20+ years in American oak butts. 93 points Wine Advocate. 12,000 bottles + 20,000 half bottles produced.
sources: https://www.vintagewineandport.co.uk/products/Bodegas-Toro-Albal%C3%A1-1999-DON-PX-1999 (Vintage Wine and Port retailer tasting note)

vintage_guidance: Non-vintage 'Rare' tier solera blend — the top of the four-tier Rutherglen Muscat classification (Rutherglen → Classic → Grand → Rare). Average age 20+ years per the Muscat of Rutherglen Network's published ladder. Multiple decades of material blended by Madden Morris (6th generation Chief Winemaker). 165-year continuous family solera since 1859. Current bottling: Decanter Platinum 2024, IWC Australian Fortified Trophy + Muscat Trophy 2025, James Halliday 100 points.
appellation: Rutherglen Muscat (Geographical Indication; Muscat of Rutherglen Network classification)
classification: Rare Rutherglen Muscat — top tier (average 20+ years, multi-decade solera blend)
selection_notes: Top-tier 'Rare' Rutherglen Muscat — the iconic Australian sweet-fortified anchor for any P3 flight. Made from Muscat à Petits Grains Rouges (Brown Muscat) grown in Rutherglen's continental North East Victoria climate, partially raisined on the vine, fortified mid-fermentation, then aged in a modified solera that draws on material spanning multiple Morris generations (winery est. 1859, currently 6th-gen Madden Morris). Provides the textbook 'olive-brown-with-green-rim, marmalade, christmas-pudding, rancio, integrated spirit' profile and lets the candidate place the wine on the four-step Rutherglen ladder. Strong curveball: shares some sensory DNA with aged Tawny Port and PX (dried fruit, rancio, sweetness) but the distinct marmalade/Christmas-cake/floral-Muscat lift is the key discriminator. Producer chosen for unrivalled medal record (Decanter Platinum 2024; IWC Australian Fortified Trophy + Muscat Trophy 2025; Halliday 100pts).

tasting_profile.appearance: Dark olive-brown to almost black at the core, with a distinctive green tinge on the rim (the hallmark Rare Rutherglen Muscat colour marker). Extremely thick and viscous in the glass -- coats the sides with an olive-brown stain when swirled. Virtually opaque.
tasting_profile.nose_summary: Intensely scented and incredibly complex. Molasses, sandalwood, chocolate-coated coffee beans, treacle tart, and baking spices (Wine-Searcher critic composite). Raisin fruit and soft woody characters. Sous bois dampened by recent rain. Perfumed but with a delicious sharpness. Restrained despite the intensity -- the components are tightly woven and harmonious rather than individually discernible. Garrigue herbs and even a touch of peach noted by Decanter.
tasting_profile.palate_summary: So thick in texture it resembles liquid raisins or Christmas pudding in a glass. Multiple layers of flavour arrive simultaneously -- rich and luscious sweet spices, dried raisins, and Christmas pudding (producer note). Dark chocolate studded with salt flakes, raisins soaked in molasses (Halliday 98pts). Incredibly sweet yet balanced by a lemony acidity and freshness throughout. Even a hint of tannin gives a dryness to the finish that counters the sweetness. Decanter rated 99/100, describing it as 'beyond fruit or chocolate flavours, mocha and espresso, garrigue herbs, powerful, sweet, almost medicinal.' The aftertaste is enormous -- one or two sips is all you need.
tasting_profile.structural_summary: Medium-plus acidity (lemony freshness providing essential balance); full body; very sweet (liqueur fortified); highly viscous, oily texture; 17% abv; exceptionally long finish that lingers for minutes. Non-vintage Rare tier solera blend, average 20+ years, multi-decade material. Modified solera, seasoned oak casks. 95 points Wine Enthusiast; 98 points James Halliday; 99/100 Decanter; 100 points Halliday (previous releases); Decanter Platinum 2024; IWC Australian Fortified Trophy + Muscat Trophy 2025.
sources: https://casellafamilybrands.com/news/halliday-wine-results (James Halliday 98pts note -- dark chocolate, salt flakes, molasses)
